events.text: The patient is a 52-year-old female named Margaret Okonkwo. She is a never-smoker with no significant alcohol use. She works as a high school English teacher. She has no family history of head and neck cancers. Her past medical history is notable for well-controlled hypothyroidism on levothyroxine, and mild gastroesophageal reflux disease. She is of Nigerian-American descent.

At age 52, the patient presented to her primary care physician with a 6-week history of progressive left-sided throat discomfort and the sensation of a lump in her throat. She noted mild odynophagia and left-sided otalgia. On examination, a firm, non-tender 2.5 cm left level II cervical lymph node was palpated. She was initially treated empirically with a 10-day course of amoxicillin-clavulanate for presumed pharyngitis. There was no cancer documented at this visit.

At age 52, approximately 5 weeks after initial presentation, the patient returned to her PCP with persistent symptoms and no improvement of the cervical lymphadenopathy despite antibiotic therapy. She was referred to otolaryngology for further evaluation. No cancer confirmed at this visit; referral placed.

At age 52, the patient underwent a CT scan of the neck with contrast as part of the otolaryngology workup. Findings showed a 2.2 cm enhancing mass centered at the left base of tongue with extension toward the left vallecula. There were multiple left-sided level II and level IIA lymph nodes, the largest measuring 2.7 cm with central necrosis, concerning for metastatic adenopathy. No contralateral lymphadenopathy. No evidence of distant metastatic disease on the chest windows. Cancer was present.

At age 52, the patient underwent flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy with directed biopsy of the left base of tongue mass under local anesthesia in the otolaryngology clinic. Pathology revealed squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated. Immunohistochemical staining showed strong diffuse nuclear and cytoplasmic p16 positivity (block-positive pattern, >70% of cells), consistent with HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. In situ hybridization for high-risk HPV RNA was positive. The specimen was negative for keratinizing morphology.

At age 52 years, the patient received a diagnosis of HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the base of tongue, left side. AJCC 8th edition staging: T2N1M0, Stage I (AJCC 8th edition for HPV-positive oropharynx). Summary stage: Regional. Primary site: Base of tongue (ICD-O-3 site code C01.9). Histology: Squamous cell carcinoma, NOS (ICD-O-3 histology code 8070/3). Tumor size: 2.2 cm. Laterality: Left. Confirmation: Positive histology. HPV/p16 status: Positive (p16+ IHC, HPV RNA ISH+). Tobacco history: Never smoker (0 pack-years). Alcohol use: None. Relevant biomarkers: p16 positive; ECOG performance status 0. The patient was referred to a multidisciplinary head and neck oncology program for treatment planning.

At age 52, the patient underwent a PET-CT scan for complete staging. FDG-PET showed an intensely hypermetabolic left base of tongue mass (SUVmax 14.2) and a hypermetabolic left level IIA lymph node measuring 2.6 cm (SUVmax 11.8) with central photopenic area consistent with necrosis. No contralateral cervical or retropharyngeal nodal involvement. No evidence of distant metastatic disease. No synchronous primary malignancy identified. Staging confirmed as T2N1M0, Stage I per AJCC 8th edition (HPV-positive oropharynx classification). Cancer was present with locoregional disease only.

At age 52, fine needle aspiration of the left level IIA lymph node was performed under ultrasound guidance. Cytology showed metastatic squamous cell carcinoma consistent with oropharyngeal primary. Cell block p16 immunostaining was strongly positive. This confirmed N1 nodal involvement as the only metastatic site.

At age 52 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the primary base of tongue biopsy. A comprehensive 500-gene panel was performed. Findings included: PIK3CA p.E545K mutation (exon 9, hotspot, variant allele frequency 18%, potentially actionable), TRAF3 p.R118* loss-of-function mutation (VAF 22%, tumor suppressor, involved in NF-kB signaling), EP300 p.R418C mutation (VAF 15%, chromatin remodeling), FBXW7 p.R505C mutation (VAF 12%), and KMT2D (MLL2) p.Q3382* truncating mutation (VAF 19%). No CDKN2A deletion. No EGFR amplification. No FGFR1 amplification. No TP53 mutation (consistent with HPV-driven carcinogenesis with functional p53 pathway suppression by E6). Copy number analysis showed focal gain of chromosome 3q (including PIK3CA locus). Tumor mutational burden: 4 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: microsatellite stable (MSS). PD-L1 CPS: 18 (by external send-out). HPV integration: detected at chromosome 8q24. No actionable fusions identified.

At age 52, the patient was discussed at the multidisciplinary head and neck tumor board. Participants included head and neck surgery, radiation oncology, medical oncology, speech pathology, nutrition/dietetics, dental oncology, and social work. Consensus recommendation was for definitive concurrent chemoradiation with intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) and cisplatin-based systemic therapy. The tumor board noted the favorable HPV-positive biology and considered de-escalation clinical trial options; however, the patient was ineligible for available trials due to the presence of necrotic lymphadenopathy (exclusion criterion on local trial). A dental evaluation was completed and all necessary extractions/prophylaxis were performed prior to radiation. Prophylactic PEG tube placement was discussed given concern for prolonged severe mucositis in a patient with baseline dysphagia. The patient elected watchful waiting on PEG placement pending early response. ECOG PS 0. Cancer present, treatment planning phase.

At age 52 years, the patient underwent percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube placement by interventional gastroenterology. This was performed electively prior to chemoradiation initiation, as a multidisciplinary decision following baseline swallowing evaluation by speech-language pathology, which showed mild-to-moderate oropharyngeal dysphagia with penetration on thin liquids. PEG was placed without complication using standard push technique under conscious sedation. The patient was counseled on tube feeding protocols and initiated supplemental enteral nutrition to optimize pre-treatment nutritional status. Pre-treatment weight: 68 kg (BMI 24.1).

At age 52 years, the patient initiated definitive intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) with simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique. Treatment plan: High-risk PTV (primary base of tongue mass and involved left level IIA node) received 70 Gy in 35 fractions (2.0 Gy/fraction). Intermediate-risk PTV (left neck levels II-IV, bilateral retropharyngeal nodes) received 63 Gy in 35 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction). Low-risk PTV (elective bilateral neck levels II-V and retropharyngeal nodes) received 56 Gy in 35 fractions (1.6 Gy/fraction). Treatment was delivered using volumetric modulated arc therapy (VMAT) with daily image guidance (CBCT). Total treatment duration: 7 weeks. Organs at risk constraints met for parotid glands (mean dose left parotid 24 Gy, right parotid 22 Gy), spinal cord (max 44 Gy), brainstem, mandible, and larynx.

At age 52 years, the patient received concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 intravenously on days 1, 22, and 43 of radiation therapy (high-dose bolus schedule). Pre-medications included aggressive IV hydration (3 L normal saline per cycle), ondansetron, dexamethasone, and magnesium supplementation. Baseline renal function: creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, GFR >60 mL/min. Audiologic baseline testing performed. The patient received 2 of 3 planned cycles; the third cycle on day 43 was dose-reduced to 75 mg/m2 due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine rise to 1.4 mg/dL) and grade 3 mucositis at the time of cycle 3.

At age 52 years, during week 4 of concurrent chemoradiation, the patient developed grade 3 oral mucositis (CTCAE v5.0) with confluent pseudomembranous ulceration involving the oropharynx and base of tongue, causing severe pain (NRS 8/10) and complete inability to tolerate oral intake. She required admission for IV hydration, IV morphine patient-controlled analgesia, and initiation of full enteral nutrition via PEG tube. She also developed grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the left neck. Weight loss from baseline: 5 kg (7.4% body weight). The patient was also noted to have mild oral candidiasis treated with oral fluconazole. PEG tube feeds were escalated to full nutritional support (Osmolite 1.5 at 65 mL/hr via continuous infusion overnight plus bolus feeds). Hospital stay: 5 days.

At age 52 years, during week 6 of chemoradiation, the patient developed grade 2 nephrotoxicity with serum creatinine rising to 1.4 mg/dL (baseline 0.8 mg/dL), attributed to cumulative cisplatin toxicity and volume depletion from mucositis-related poor oral intake. Aggressive IV hydration was administered on an outpatient infusion basis. Magnesium wasting requiring IV magnesium repletion was noted (serum Mg 1.3 mg/dL). Additionally, grade 2 fatigue was present. No significant ototoxicity on repeat audiologic evaluation. Grade 1 xerostomia developing. The patient continued radiation without interruption throughout the treatment course.

At age 52, end-of-treatment assessment was performed at completion of chemoradiation (week 7, day 35 of radiation). The patient completed all 35 fractions of IMRT as planned. She received 2 full cycles of cisplatin and 1 dose-reduced cycle (75 mg/m2). She tolerated treatment with the toxicities as documented. She remained on full PEG tube feeds at treatment completion. Oral mucositis was improving (now grade 2). Weight at completion: 63 kg (loss of 5 kg total from pre-treatment baseline). ECOG PS 2 at end of treatment. Clinical examination showed no palpable residual cervical lymphadenopathy. The primary site was not evaluable by clinical exam during the acute mucositis period. The patient was advised that response assessment imaging should be deferred 8-12 weeks post-treatment to allow resolution of radiation-induced inflammatory changes. Cancer status: uncertain at this time, post-treatment evaluation pending.

At age 52, approximately 10 weeks following completion of chemoradiation, the patient underwent a PET-CT scan for post-treatment response assessment. Findings: Complete metabolic response at the primary left base of tongue site - no residual FDG-avid disease (previously SUVmax 14.2, now no discernible uptake above background). Complete metabolic response at the left level IIA lymph node - previously necrotic and hypermetabolic node now shows residual fibrotic nodal tissue without FDG uptake (SUVmax 1.8, below mediastinal blood pool threshold). No new sites of FDG-avid disease. No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Impression: Complete metabolic response (CMR) per PERCIST 1.0 criteria. Cancer was absent.

At age 52 (approximately 10 weeks post-chemoradiation), the patient was seen in multidisciplinary follow-up. PET-CT confirmed complete metabolic response. On clinical examination, no palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. Flexible laryngoscopy showed healing mucosa at the base of tongue with no gross residual tumor. The patient was tolerating soft foods and thickened liquids orally, though still requiring supplemental PEG tube feeds for approximately 50% of her caloric needs. Xerostomia: grade 1-2 (troublesome but manageable). No trismus. Hypothyroidism stable on levothyroxine; TSH was within normal range on repeat labs. Creatinine had returned to 1.0 mg/dL. The patient was counseled on complete response and favorable prognosis. The multidisciplinary team discussed the concept of de-escalation monitoring given HPV-positive biology and complete metabolic response. Cancer absent; complete response confirmed.

At age 52 (3 months post-chemoradiation), the patient was seen for routine surveillance. She had achieved full oral intake and the PEG tube had been removed 2 weeks prior without complication. Weight recovering: 65 kg. She continued to experience grade 1 xerostomia and mild dysphagia to solids, managed with salivary substitutes and dietary modification. She had resumed teaching duties part-time. Speech and swallowing therapy ongoing twice weekly. No new symptoms. Physical examination: no palpable lymphadenopathy, healing oropharyngeal mucosa without suspicious lesions on flexible laryngoscopy. Labs: CBC, CMP all within normal limits; TSH 1.8 (normal). ECOG PS 1. No evidence of disease.

At age 52 (6 months post-chemoradiation), the patient underwent a surveillance CT scan of the neck and chest with contrast. Findings showed no evidence of local recurrence at the base of tongue. The previously involved left level IIA lymph node was now reduced to a small fibrotic remnant measuring 8 mm (previously 2.7 cm at diagnosis), with no suspicious enhancement or necrosis. No new cervical, retropharyngeal, or mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Chest: no pulmonary nodules or masses concerning for metastatic disease. No new findings. Impression: No evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease. Cancer was absent.

At age 52-53 (6-month surveillance visit post-chemoradiation), the patient was seen in head and neck oncology follow-up. She reported continued improvement in swallowing. Xerostomia remained grade 1. She had resumed full-time work as a teacher. She denied throat pain, odynophagia, new neck masses, weight loss, or otalgia. Flexible laryngoscopy: base of tongue with smooth mucosa, no ulceration or suspicious lesion. No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. The treating team discussed the ongoing favorable trajectory and noted that per emerging data on HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer surveillance, there is no proven benefit to PET-CT beyond 3-6 months post-treatment for patients with complete metabolic response. Clinical surveillance with quarterly clinical examination and selective use of CT was planned. No evidence of disease.

At age 53 (8 months post-chemoradiation), the patient presented with a new complaint of progressive left-sided neck stiffness and mild pain. On examination, there was soft tissue induration of the left neck consistent with radiation fibrosis (grade 2 late radiation fibrosis by LENT-SOMA criteria). No mass was palpable. Range of neck motion was mildly reduced. She was referred to physical therapy for cervical range-of-motion exercises and myofascial release. Additionally, she was noted to have grade 1 hypothyroidism worsening (TSH 7.2), attributed to radiation-induced hypothyroidism, and levothyroxine dose was increased from 75 mcg to 100 mcg daily.

At age 53 (12 months post-chemoradiation), the patient underwent a surveillance CT scan of the neck and chest with contrast at the 1-year mark. Findings: No evidence of local tumor recurrence at the primary base of tongue site. Minimal residual fibrotic tissue in the left level IIA nodal region, stable compared to prior imaging. No suspicious lymphadenopathy in the neck or mediastinum. Chest: No pulmonary metastases. Mild radiation-related changes in the left submandibular gland region. Impression: No evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease. Cancer was absent.

At age 53 (12-month post-treatment surveillance visit), the patient was seen for annual assessment. She was doing well overall. Swallowing function had further improved, though she noted mild dysphagia to hard, dry solids (e.g., raw carrots, crackers). Modified barium swallow study performed by speech-language pathology showed mild pharyngeal dysmotility with reduced base-of-tongue retraction, no aspiration, and trace penetration managed with chin-tuck maneuver. Xerostomia: stable at grade 1, using biotene spray and xylitol lozenges with benefit. Neck fibrosis improving with physical therapy. TSH now 2.1 on adjusted levothyroxine dose. ECOG PS 0. She was counseled on the excellent prognosis for HPV-positive stage III oropharyngeal cancer with complete response, with estimated 5-year disease-specific survival exceeding 85%. No evidence of disease.

At age 53-54 (18 months post-chemoradiation), the patient underwent a surveillance CT of the neck with contrast as part of ongoing surveillance protocol. CT neck showed no evidence of local recurrence. The left neck fibrotic nodal remnant was stable and non-suspicious. No new lymphadenopathy. No evidence of distant disease on limited chest survey. Cancer was absent.

At age 54 (18-month surveillance visit), the patient was seen in head and neck oncology and medical oncology co-management. She reported overall excellent quality of life. Dysphagia had continued to improve and she was tolerating most foods with modifications. She was fully employed as a teacher. No concerning symptoms. Physical exam: No palpable neck masses or lymphadenopathy. Flexible laryngoscopy unremarkable. The multidisciplinary team reviewed the de-escalation literature, including ORATOR2 trial data and NRG-HN002, noting that while de-escalation had been explored in similar low-risk HPV-positive patients (non-smokers, T2N1, complete metabolic response), this patient received standard-of-care treatment and was now in surveillance. Ongoing surveillance was planned at 6-month intervals for years 2-3, then annually thereafter. Dental surveillance and fluoride treatments ongoing for radiation-induced caries prevention. No evidence of disease.

At age 54 (24 months post-chemoradiation, 2-year surveillance), the patient underwent comprehensive 2-year assessment. She reported excellent functional recovery. Swallowing was near-normal. Xerostomia was grade 1 (mild, not affecting quality of life significantly). She had gained back all treatment-related weight loss (weight: 68.5 kg). ECOG PS 0. Flexible laryngoscopy: No suspicious mucosal lesions at the base of tongue. No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. TSH: 1.9 (normal on current levothyroxine dose). CBC and comprehensive metabolic panel within normal limits. She was counseled that at 2 years post-treatment with no evidence of recurrence, the risk of late recurrence was significantly reduced, and the likelihood of cure was high. The patient expressed gratitude and interest in participating in a survivorship program and HPV-positive head and neck cancer support group. Surveillance interval extended to every 6 months. No evidence of disease; durable complete response at 2 years.

documents.text:
PRIMARY CARE FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Primary Care Physician]
DEPARTMENT: Internal Medicine / Primary Care
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient Follow-Up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Follow-up for persistent left-sided throat discomfort and cervical lymphadenopathy; no improvement after 10-day course of amoxicillin-clavulanate.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 52-year-old Nigerian-American female, high school English teacher, never-smoker with no significant alcohol use, who returns to clinic approximately 5 weeks after her initial presentation for reassessment of persistent left-sided throat symptoms and cervical lymphadenopathy. At her prior visit, she described a 6-week history of progressive left-sided throat discomfort with a globus sensation, mild odynophagia, and left-sided referred otalgia. At that time, a firm, non-tender 2.5 cm left level II cervical lymph node was palpated. She was empirically treated with amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 10 days for a presumptive diagnosis of bacterial pharyngitis or cervical lymphadenitis.

She has now completed that course of antibiotics and reports no subjective improvement whatsoever. The left-sided throat discomfort persists and she describes it as a persistent dull ache with occasional sharp pain on swallowing, particularly with solid foods. The globus sensation — the feeling of something lodged in the back of her throat — is unchanged. The left otalgia has not resolved; she describes it as a deep, intermittent aching sensation in the left ear without hearing loss or otorrhea. She denies fever, chills, or night sweats currently, though she notes she had some mild low-grade fevers (self-measured at approximately 99.5°F) in the first week of her antibiotic course. She denies rigors. She has not noticed any new lumps or bumps elsewhere in the neck, axillae, or groin.

She denies any significant unintentional weight loss, though she acknowledges her appetite has been slightly reduced over the past few weeks due to the discomfort with eating. She denies any voice changes, hoarseness, or change in speech. She denies hemoptysis or coughing up blood. She denies any trismus or difficulty opening her mouth. She has not noted any skin changes over the neck mass.

She does report that the neck node itself feels the same size to her — possibly even slightly larger, though she acknowledges uncertainty about this subjective assessment. She has been using ibuprofen 400 mg intermittently for pain relief with partial effect.

Relevant social and occupational history: She is a high school English teacher, currently in the middle of the academic year, and is concerned about her ongoing symptoms affecting her ability to speak clearly and project her voice in the classroom. She reports significant anxiety about the persistent nature of these symptoms and is worried about what they might mean. She specifically asks whether this could be "something more serious."

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Positive: Left-sided throat discomfort/otalgia as described above. Mild odynophagia with solid foods. Globus sensation. Mildly reduced appetite. Intermittent low-grade fevers (resolved). Mild anxiety and concern about the diagnosis.
Negative: No high fevers currently. No rigors or night sweats. No significant unintentional weight loss. No hoarseness or dysphonia. No hemoptysis. No shortness of breath or dyspnea. No chest pain. No skin lesions or rashes. No trismus or limited mouth opening. No vision changes. No neurologic symptoms. No axillary or inguinal lymphadenopathy noted by patient. No recent upper respiratory tract infection in close contacts. No sick contacts with confirmed infectious illness. No recent travel outside the United States. No new sexual partners; she is in a long-term monogamous relationship. No history of oral cavity lesions, leukoplakia, or recurrent oral ulcers. GERD symptoms stable on current management (dietary modifications, no new PPI use). Hypothyroidism stable; no new symptoms of thyroid dysfunction (no palpitations, no heat intolerance, no new weight change beyond as described).

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Hypothyroidism — well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; last TSH checked approximately 8 months ago, within normal limits.
2. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) — mild, managed with dietary modification and occasional antacid use; no current PPI therapy.
3. No prior head and neck infections requiring hospitalization.
4. No prior ENT procedures.
5. No prior malignancy.

SURGICAL HISTORY:
No prior surgeries.

FAMILY HISTORY:
No family history of head and neck cancers. No family history of lymphoma or hematologic malignancy. Mother: hypertension, type 2 diabetes. Father: died in Nigeria from cardiovascular disease. No siblings with known malignancies.

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Never-smoker. Rare social alcohol use — patient reports essentially no current alcohol consumption. Married; two adult children in college. Works as a high school English teacher; moderate voice demands professionally. No occupational exposures to known carcinogens. Born in Nigeria; immigrated to the United States as a young adult. Does not use recreational drugs or tobacco products including e-cigarettes or smokeless tobacco. Sexually active in a long-term monogamous relationship.

ALLERGIES:
NKDA. No known drug allergies. Tolerates amoxicillin-clavulanate without reported allergic reaction (completed full course).

MEDICATIONS:
1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally every morning on an empty stomach — for hypothyroidism.
2. Ibuprofen 400 mg orally as needed for pain (intermittent use, not daily).
3. Occasional calcium carbonate antacid as needed for GERD symptoms.
(Completed course: amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg BID x 10 days — no longer taking.)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs:
Blood pressure: 118/74 mmHg | Heart rate: 76 bpm, regular | Respiratory rate: 16 breaths/min | Temperature: 98.6°F (37.0°C) | SpO2: 98% on room air | Weight: 68 kg | BMI: 24.1 kg/m²

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished 52-year-old woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Appropriately dressed. She is mildly anxious in affect, asking questions about the significance of her symptoms throughout the exam.

Head: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No scalp lesions. No facial asymmetry.

Eyes: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light. No scleral icterus. Extraocular movements intact.

Ears: External auditory canals clear bilaterally. Tympanic membranes intact and translucent bilaterally. No middle ear effusion, no erythema. Hearing grossly intact to finger rub bilaterally.

Nose: No septal deviation. No discharge. No evidence of nasal polyposis on anterior rhinoscopy.

Oral Cavity / Oropharynx: Oral mucosa moist and intact. No aphthous ulcers, no leukoplakia, no erythroplakia. Dentition in fair condition; no obvious periapical disease. Tongue midline, no lesions on the oral tongue visible. Tonsils: bilaterally present, 1+, no tonsillar exudates or asymmetry noted on oropharyngeal inspection with tongue depressor. Posterior pharyngeal wall appears mildly erythematous. The base of tongue was not adequately assessed on direct inspection (requires flexible or mirror laryngoscopy for adequate visualization). Uvula midline.

Neck:
- Left level II cervical lymph node: firm, non-tender, approximately 2.5 cm in greatest dimension, mobile relative to overlying skin but appearing adherent to deeper structures when manipulated. No fluctuance, no overlying skin erythema, no warmth. The node appears at minimum unchanged in size compared to the prior visit 5 weeks ago; it may be marginally larger but this is difficult to gauge precisely on clinical exam.
- No right-sided cervical lymphadenopathy appreciated.
- No supraclavicular, infraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy detected.
- Thyroid: No palpable goiter, no nodules detected on palpation.
- No midline neck masses.
- No tenderness on palpation of the submandibular or parotid regions.

Respiratory: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Breathing unlabored.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly on palpation. No inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Skin: No cutaneous masses, no suspicious nevi, no petechiae or purpura.

Neurologic: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact as assessed in the office. No facial weakness, no tongue deviation, no shoulder shrug asymmetry.

---

LAB RESULTS:
Prior labs reviewed. No labs drawn at today's visit. At initial visit, no specific labs were obtained. For context, the patient's most recent TSH (approximately 8 months ago) was within normal limits.

No CBC, CRP, ESR, or Monospot was obtained at the initial visit. These were not checked prior to the empiric antibiotic course. No LDH, ferritin, or other baseline labs are available for review.

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
No prior imaging has been obtained for this complaint to date.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. PERSISTENT LEFT CERVICAL LYMPHADENOPATHY, UNRESPONSIVE TO ANTIBIOTICS — CONCERNING FOR NEOPLASTIC ETIOLOGY:
Ms. Okonkwo presents with a firm, non-tender, persistent level II cervical lymph node that has not responded to a full course of broad-spectrum antibiotic therapy over a 5-week period. The clinical characteristics of this node are concerning: size greater than 2 cm, firm consistency, relative fixation to deeper structures, and persistence despite antibiotic therapy. Combined with her persistent left-sided throat symptoms and referred left otalgia — which can be a sign of a hypopharyngeal or base-of-tongue lesion irritating the glossopharyngeal or vagus nerve — this presentation raises significant concern for an underlying malignancy, most likely a primary head and neck squamous cell carcinoma or possibly lymphoma.

It is worth noting that while she is a never-smoker and has no alcohol use — the classic risk factors for head and neck SCC — oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma driven by HPV infection has become the predominant etiology in non-smoking patients of this age demographic. The base of tongue and tonsils are the most common primary sites for HPV-driven oropharyngeal cancer, and a base of tongue primary can be very difficult to visualize without flexible laryngoscopy. The referred otalgia she describes is particularly notable, as CN IX-mediated referred ear pain is a recognized presentation of hypopharyngeal and oropharyngeal pathology.

The decision to treat empirically with antibiotics at the initial visit was reasonable given the initial presentation; however, with no response after 10 days of amoxicillin-clavulanate and now 5 weeks of total symptom duration with a firm, persistent level II node, empiric antibiotic extension would not be appropriate. This requires urgent ENT evaluation for flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy, direct visualization of the base of tongue, vallecula, hypopharynx, and larynx, and likely tissue biopsy.

Plan:
- Urgent referral placed today to otolaryngology (ENT/head and neck surgery) for comprehensive evaluation including flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy. Request marked as urgent, with preference for appointment within 1-2 weeks.
- I have discussed with the patient the reason for this referral and have been transparent with her that while we do not have a diagnosis, the persistent lymphadenopathy requires a thorough workup to rule out a serious underlying cause including cancer. She was understandably distressed by this conversation; I spent additional time addressing her concerns and questions. She is accompanied by her husband today and I provided information to both of them.
- I will order CT of the neck with contrast to be obtained prior to or concurrent with the otolaryngology evaluation to better characterize the extent of lymphadenopathy and to evaluate for a potential primary lesion. This imaging will be available for the ENT team at the time of consultation.
- If lymphoma is suspected by the ENT team, referral to hematology/oncology will be facilitated.
- No further antibiotics prescribed; there is no clinical evidence of a bacterial process and extending empiric therapy further is not indicated.

2. PAIN MANAGEMENT:
- Continue ibuprofen 400-600 mg as needed for throat and ear discomfort. Counsel regarding use with food given GERD history.
- If ibuprofen is inadequate, may consider short course of acetaminophen 500-1000 mg TID as an alternative or adjunct.

3. HYPOTHYROIDISM:
- Stable per patient report. No medication changes at this time.
- TSH should be rechecked at next routine follow-up given it has been approximately 8 months since last check.

4. GERD:
- No new symptoms. No medication changes.

5. PATIENT COMMUNICATION AND RETURN PRECAUTIONS:
- The patient was counseled extensively today. She understands the concern and the reason for the urgent ENT referral. She voiced understanding and agreement with the plan.
- I emphasized the importance of attending the ENT appointment promptly and provided direct contact information for questions.
- Return precautions reviewed: she was advised to return to the ED or call the office urgently if she develops sudden worsening of dysphagia, any difficulty breathing, stridor, significant voice change, high fever, or a rapidly enlarging neck mass.
- I will personally follow up by telephone within 1 week to confirm the ENT appointment has been scheduled.

Visit complexity: High (detailed medical decision-making, significant counseling time, coordination of specialist referral and imaging).

Time spent in face-to-face counseling and coordination: approximately 30 minutes total visit time.

[Electronically signed by attending physician of record]

HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY / RADIATION ONCOLOGY
END-OF-TREATMENT NOTE — COMPLETION OF DEFINITIVE CHEMORADIATION

ATTENDING: Head and Neck Medical Oncology / Radiation Oncology (Co-signed)
VISIT TYPE: End-of-Treatment Assessment, In-Person

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Completion of definitive concurrent chemoradiation for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, base of tongue. End-of-treatment assessment and post-treatment planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 52-year-old Nigerian-American female, never-smoker, who presented initially with a 6-week history of progressive left-sided throat discomfort, globus sensation, odynophagia, and referred left-sided otalgia. She was found to have a 2.2 cm left base of tongue mass with left level IIA cervical lymphadenopathy (largest node 2.7 cm with central necrosis on CT). Biopsy confirmed HPV-positive (p16+, HPV RNA ISH+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, staged as T2N1M0, Stage I per AJCC 8th edition (HPV-positive oropharynx classification). Comprehensive staging PET-CT demonstrated a hypermetabolic left base of tongue mass (SUVmax 14.2) and hypermetabolic left level IIA node (SUVmax 11.8) without evidence of distant metastatic disease. NGS of the primary tumor showed a PIK3CA E545K mutation, TRAF3 loss-of-function, and other co-mutations, with low tumor mutational burden (4 mt/Mb), MSS status, and PD-L1 CPS 18. Following discussion at multidisciplinary tumor board, the patient proceeded with definitive concurrent chemoradiation given her excellent performance status and the absence of eligible de-escalation trial enrollment (exclusion due to necrotic nodal disease).

The patient completed all 35 fractions of IMRT using a simultaneous integrated boost (SIB-VMAT) technique today, marking the end of her 7-week treatment course. The treatment fields encompassed the left base of tongue primary and left level IIA node (high-risk PTV, 70 Gy/35 fractions at 2.0 Gy/fx), left neck levels II-IV and bilateral retropharyngeal nodes (intermediate-risk PTV, 63 Gy/35 fractions at 1.8 Gy/fx), and elective bilateral neck levels II-V (low-risk PTV, 56 Gy/35 fractions at 1.6 Gy/fx). Concurrent systemic therapy was administered as high-dose bolus cis 100 mg/m2 on days 1, 22, and 43. Due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine rise to 1.4 mg/dL from baseline 0.8 mg/dL) and grade 3 mucositis at the time of cycle 3 (week 6), the third Platinol dose was administered at a reduced dose of 75 mg/m2. In total, she received 2 full cycles and 1 dose-reduced cycle of Platinol.

The treatment course was complicated by several significant toxicities. During week 4, the patient developed severe grade 3 oral mucositis with confluent pseudomembranous ulceration of the oropharynx and base of tongue, resulting in complete inability to tolerate oral intake and pain rated 8/10. She required a 5-day hospital admission for IV hydration, IV morphine via patient-controlled analgesia, and escalation to full enteral nutrition via the PEG tube (Osmolite 1.5 at 65 mL/hr continuous overnight plus bolus feeds). Oral candidiasis was identified during that admission and treated with fluconazole. Additionally, she developed grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the left neck during week 5-6, and grade 2 nephrotoxicity during week 6 as noted above, requiring outpatient IV hydration and magnesium repletion (nadir serum Mg 1.3 mg/dL). She also experienced grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 xerostomia. Radiation was delivered without interruption throughout the entire 7-week course despite these toxicities, which is commendable. She is currently maintained on full PEG tube feeds.

Pre-treatment weight was 68 kg (BMI 24.1). She has experienced approximately 5 kg total weight loss over the treatment course (current weight 63 kg).

PET-CT SCAN WITH FDG (WHOLE BODY) - POST-TREATMENT RESPONSE ASSESSMENT

EXAMINATION: FDG-PET/CT, whole body from vertex to mid-thigh

INDICATION: 52-year-old female with HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition, Stage I, HPV-positive classification). Treated with definitive concurrent chemoradiation: intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) with simultaneous integrated boost to a total dose of 70 Gy in 35 fractions, delivered over 7 weeks, concurrent with Platinol 100 mg/m2 (2 full cycles plus 1 dose-reduced cycle at 75 mg/m2). This study is performed approximately 10 weeks following the final radiation fraction for post-treatment metabolic response assessment. Comparison is made to the pre-treatment staging PET-CT.

TECHNIQUE: Intravenous injection of 11.4 mCi (421 MBq) of F-18 fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG). Images were acquired 62 minutes following radiotracer injection. Blood glucose at time of injection: 91 mg/dL (within acceptable range). Whole-body PET emission data was acquired in 3D mode from vertex to mid-thigh and fused with simultaneously acquired low-dose CT data used for attenuation correction and anatomic localization. IV iodinated contrast was administered for the diagnostic CT component. Oral contrast was administered. The study was reviewed on dedicated PET and CT workstations with multiplanar reconstructions and co-registered fusion images. Maximum standardized uptake values (SUVmax) are reported using lean body mass correction.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Margaret Okonkwo is a 52-year-old Nigerian-American female, never-smoker, with HPV-positive (p16+, HPV RNA ISH+) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue (T2N1M0, Stage I per AJCC 8th edition, HPV-positive oropharynx classification) who presents today for her formal post-treatment response assessment, approximately 10 weeks following completion of definitive concurrent chemoradiation therapy (IMRT with cis).

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo originally presented approximately seven months ago with a 6-week history of progressive left-sided throat discomfort and globus sensation, left-sided otalgia, and mild odynophagia. Workup at that time revealed a 2.2 cm enhancing left base of tongue mass on CT with multiple left-sided level II lymph nodes including one measuring 2.7 cm with central necrosis. Pathology from a directed biopsy under laryngoscopy confirmed moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with strong diffuse p16 positivity (>70% of cells, block-positive) and positive HPV RNA by in situ hybridization. Fine needle aspiration of the dominant left level IIA node confirmed metastatic squamous cell carcinoma with p16 positivity. PET-CT staging demonstrated a hypermetabolic left base of tongue primary (SUVmax 14.2) and a hypermetabolic, necrotic left level IIA node (SUVmax 11.8) with no evidence of contralateral nodal disease, distant metastases, or synchronous primary malignancy. NGS on the primary specimen identified a PIK3CA p.E545K hotspot mutation, TRAF3 loss-of-function mutation, and KMT2D truncating mutation among other co-alterations; TMB was low (4 mut/Mb) and PD-L1 CPS was 18. The patient was staged as T2N1M0 Stage I (AJCC 8th edition, HPV-positive oropharynx).

Following multidisciplinary tumor board review — which considered de-escalation clinical trial enrollment but found the patient ineligible due to the presence of necrotic lymphadenopathy — the decision was made to proceed with standard-of-care definitive IMRT using a simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique with concurrent high-dose cis. Prior to treatment, a percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube was placed prophylactically given a pre-treatment swallowing evaluation demonstrating mild-to-moderate oropharyngeal dysphagia with penetration on thin liquids.

The patient received 70 Gy in 35 fractions (2.0 Gy/fraction) to the high-risk planning target volume encompassing the primary and the involved left level IIA node, with intermediate and low-risk fields treated to 63 Gy and 56 Gy respectively using VMAT with daily CBCT image guidance. She completed all 35 fractions without interruption.

Concurrent systemic therapy consisted of cis 100 mg/m2 on days 1, 22, and 43. The day 43 cycle was dose-reduced to 75 mg/m2 due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine 1.4 mg/dL from a baseline of 0.8 mg/dL) and co-occurring grade 3 oral mucositis at the time of that cycle. She received 2 full cycles and 1 dose-reduced cycle, for a cumulative Platinol dose of approximately 275 mg/m2.

Toxicities during treatment were significant. She developed grade 3 confluent pseudomembranous oral mucositis during week 4, requiring a 5-day inpatient admission for IV hydration, IV morphine PCA, and initiation of full enteral nutrition via PEG. She also had grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the left neck and grade 2 nephrotoxicity with hypomagnesemia requiring IV repletion. Despite these toxicities, radiation was not interrupted. At completion of treatment, the patient weighed 63 kg (down 5 kg from her pre-treatment baseline of 68 kg), was ECOG PS 2, and was still requiring full PEG tube feeds.

Today, approximately 10 weeks post-completion of chemoradiation, she returns for her post-treatment response assessment. She was advised at her end-of-treatment visit to defer imaging by 8-12 weeks to allow inflammatory changes to subside before attempting response evaluation. A PET-CT was performed last week and the results are reviewed today.

Ms. Okonkwo reports that overall she is feeling significantly better than at the completion of treatment. The oral mucositis has largely resolved, and she is now tolerating soft foods and thickened liquids by mouth. She continues to use the PEG tube for supplemental nutrition — approximately half of her daily caloric intake — but notes this is a major improvement from full tube dependence at end of treatment. She continues to experience xerostomia, which she describes as bothersome but manageable, worse in the mornings and when speaking for long periods in class. She notes significant fatigue that is slowly improving week to week. She denies any new throat pain, neck masses, bleeding, hemoptysis, unintentional weight loss beyond her treatment-related loss, or new neurological symptoms. She has not returned to full-time teaching yet but is beginning to feel more like herself. She has been using salivary substitutes with partial relief.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Okonkwo is a 52-year-old never-smoking Nigerian-American woman with HPV-positive (p16+, HPV RNA ISH+) moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue (T2N1M0 per AJCC 8th edition), who has completed definitive concurrent chemoradiation (70 Gy IMRT in 35 fractions + 2 full cycles and 1 dose-reduced cycle of cis 100 mg/m2). She is now approximately 10 weeks post-treatment and presents today for her formal response assessment.

1. COMPLETE RESPONSE CONFIRMED — EXCELLENT NEWS
The post-treatment PET-CT demonstrates a complete metabolic response at both the primary site and the involved left level IIA lymph node. Flexible laryngoscopy today shows no gross residual primary tumor. Clinically, there is no palpable residual cervical lymphadenopathy. Together, these findings represent a complete clinical, endoscopic, and metabolic response.

This is the expected best-case outcome for a patient with HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC treated with definitive chemoradiation and is fully consistent with the favorable biology of this disease. In the setting of stage I (T2N1M0) HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer with complete metabolic response in a never-smoking patient, the expected 5-year disease-specific survival is excellent — data from NRG/RTOG trials and institutional series consistently show survival rates in the range of 80-90% or better for this population.

The patient was counseled extensively regarding these findings. She understands that complete response on PET-CT does not guarantee cure but represents the best possible early outcome following treatment. She will enter a structured surveillance program with the goal of detecting any late recurrence as early as possible.

2. TREATMENT-RELATED TOXICITY RECOVERY — IMPROVING
- Mucositis: Largely resolved (now approximately grade 1 at most), consistent with expected recovery trajectory at 10 weeks post-IMRT. No intervention required today.
- Renal function: Creatinine returned to 1.0 mg/dL (near baseline of 0.8). This is reassuring given the grade 2 nephrotoxicity encountered during treatment. Will continue to monitor at each visit. No nephrology referral needed at this time. She should remain well-hydrated and avoid nephrotoxic agents.
- Hypomagnesemia: Resolved. Magnesium 1.9 mg/dL today. No supplementation required.
- Xerostomia: Grade 1-2. She is managing with Biotene spray and oral lubricants. Will continue these as needed. Pilocarpine discussed as a pharmacological option for more severe or refractory xerostomia — will hold for now given tolerability, but can revisit if symptoms worsen or plateau without improvement.
- Dysphagia: Currently tolerating soft foods and thickened liquids. Excellent trajectory. She remains on supplemental PEG tube feeds at ~50% of caloric needs, which is appropriate at this stage. Continue aggressive speech-language pathology-directed swallowing therapy with the goal of transitioning to full oral intake and eventual PEG removal.
- Nutritional status: Weight 64.2 kg, albumin 3.7. Nutritional recovery underway. Continue current enteral supplementation and dietary counseling. Aim to advance to full oral intake as swallowing function permits.
- Anemia: Mild (Hgb 11.8 g/dL). Likely multifactorial — post-treatment bone marrow suppression and nutritional. Monitor at next visit; iron studies and B12/folate will be checked at 3-month follow-up if not improved. No transfusion indicated.
- Fatigue: Grade 1-2, improving. Counsel on gentle progressive physical activity as tolerated. Consider formal cancer rehabilitation referral if fatigue limits functional recovery at the 3-month visit.

3. HYPOTHYROIDISM MONITORING
TSH is 2.4 today — normal on current levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. However, radiation-induced hypothyroidism is well-described following neck irradiation and may evolve over months to years post-treatment. Will monitor TSH every 3 months. Low threshold to up-titrate levothyroxine if TSH begins to rise.

4. REFLUX MANAGEMENT
Will continue omeprazole 20 mg daily. Ongoing GERD management is particularly important in a patient with post-radiation oropharyngeal/esophageal changes, as reflux can worsen mucositis and dysphagia recovery.

5. DENTAL AND ORAL HEALTH
Patient should be seen by dental oncology for ongoing fluoride trays and caries surveillance. Radiation to the salivary glands markedly increases the risk of radiation-induced dental caries, which can in turn create a risk for osteoradionecrosis if extractions are needed in the future. Reinforce meticulous oral hygiene, daily prescription fluoride use, and avoidance of invasive dental procedures in irradiated bone without consultation with the radiation oncology team.

6. SURVEILLANCE PLAN
Given complete response, the patient will enter a standard surveillance protocol for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC. We discussed the data suggesting that in patients with a complete metabolic response on PET-CT, routine PET imaging beyond this initial post-treatment scan has not been shown to improve outcomes or detect recurrence at a stage when it would be more salvageable in most HPV-positive patients. Therefore, our surveillance plan moving forward will rely primarily on clinical examination and selective CT imaging rather than serial PET-CT.

Surveillance schedule:
- Every 3 months for the first 2 years (clinical exam, flexible laryngoscopy)
- CT neck and chest with contrast at 6 months post-treatment and 12 months post-treatment
- TSH every 3 months for the first year, then every 6 months
- Return to clinic in approximately 3 months (approximately the 3-month post-treatment mark)
- Annual imaging and/or clinical examination thereafter per NCCN head and neck cancer surveillance guidelines

Patient was asked about interest in HPV-positive head and neck cancer survivorship programs and peer support groups. She expressed strong interest. Referral placed to our institutional survivorship clinic.

Return to work as a teacher: She is currently at approximately 50% capacity. Encouraged to gradually increase as energy and voice allow. If voice fatigue or dysphonia becomes a significant occupational issue, otolaryngology/speech pathology referral for voice therapy will be arranged.

7. PATIENT EDUCATION AND COUNSELING
Extensive time was spent with Ms. Okonkwo today discussing her response and what it means for her long-term prognosis. She asked thoughtful and insightful questions about recurrence risk, what symptoms should prompt urgent re-evaluation, and what the data show about HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer in the current era. We emphasized the following:
- Symptoms to watch for and report promptly: new neck lumps or swelling, return of throat pain or odynophagia, hemoptysis, ear pain, new dysphagia beyond current baseline, unexplained weight loss.
- The HPV-positive designation carries favorable implications, and her complete metabolic response is the best possible imaging outcome post-treatment.
- Long-term toxicity monitoring (thyroid, dentition, swallowing, hearing, renal function) is an important and ongoing part of her survivorship care.

Ms. Okonkwo expressed relief and emotion at the news of complete response. She was engaged, asking appropriate questions, and verbalized understanding of the surveillance plan. Total visit time: approximately 60 minutes.

IMPRESSION AND PLAN

1. PERSISTENT LEFT CERVICAL LYMPHADENOPATHY — FAILURE TO RESPOND TO ANTIBIOTICS, CONCERN FOR MALIGNANCY
Ms. Okonkwo presents with an 11-week history of progressive left-sided throat discomfort and a persistent firm level II cervical lymph node measuring approximately 2.5–3 cm that has not diminished following a full course of amoxicillin-clavulanate. The combination of: (a) a non-tender firm cervical lymph node at level II in an adult, (b) persistence and possible slight enlargement despite antibiotics, (c) associated ipsilateral referred otalgia — which is a well-recognized symptom of base-of-tongue and tonsillar pathology due to referred pain via the glossopharyngeal nerve (Arnold's nerve reflex) — and (d) prolonged odynophagia, raises significant concern for an oropharyngeal or hypopharyngeal malignancy with regional lymph node involvement.

Although the patient is a never-smoker and non-drinker — which reduces her traditional risk for squamous cell carcinoma — it is important to recognize that HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma is increasingly common in precisely this demographic: non-smoking, otherwise healthy, middle-aged adults. Level II lymphadenopathy in this setting is a classic presentation of oropharyngeal primary malignancy (base of tongue, tonsil).

The differential at this time includes:
   - Oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (most concerning given the clinical picture)
   - Lymphoma (less likely but cannot be excluded given this node has not responded to antibiotics)
   - Reactive lymphadenopathy from a non-infectious inflammatory process
   - Infectious etiology (e.g., EBV mononucleosis, atypical mycobacterium, toxoplasmosis) — now less likely given antibiotic failure and duration
   - Thyroid malignancy with lateral neck metastasis — less likely given the clinically normal thyroid
   - Salivary gland neoplasm — possible but less likely

Plan:
   a. Urgent referral to Otolaryngology/Head and Neck Surgery placed today. The referral specifically notes: persistent 2.5–3 cm left level II cervical lymph node, failure to respond to antibiotics, associated ipsilateral otalgia and odynophagia, without obvious infectious source, in a 52-year-old never-smoking woman. The referral is marked URGENT, with the aim of an appointment within 1–2 weeks.
   b. CT scan of the neck with contrast is ordered today in anticipation of the otolaryngology workup, both to characterize the lymph node further and to evaluate the oropharynx, hypopharynx, and parapharyngeal space for a primary lesion. The imaging is ordered on an expedited basis.
   c. The patient is instructed to go directly to the Emergency Department if she develops any acute dyspnea, progressive dysphagia to liquids, stridor, or hemoptysis prior to her specialist appointment.
   d. No further empirical antibiotics prescribed at this time — there is no clinical indication for a second course and further antibiotic delay risks delaying diagnosis.

2. HYPOTHYROIDISM

3. GERD

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**
Routine 6-month post-treatment surveillance for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue.

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**

Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 52-year-old Nigerian-American woman and never-smoker with no significant alcohol history who originally presented with a 6-week history of progressive left-sided throat discomfort, odynophagia, left-sided otalgia, and a firm 2.5 cm left level II cervical lymph node. Workup confirmed a 2.2 cm left base of tongue mass with a 2.6 cm left level IIA necrotic lymph node. Biopsy demonstrated moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with strong diffuse p16 positivity and confirmatory high-risk HPV RNA in situ hybridization positivity, establishing a diagnosis of HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (AJCC 8th edition T2N1M0, Stage I; noting the registry also lists this as Stage III per older classification schema). Comprehensive NGS from the primary specimen showed a PIK3CA p.E545K hotspot mutation, TRAF3 loss-of-function mutation, and several chromatin-remodeling alterations (EP300, KMT2D), consistent with HPV-associated molecular biology. No TP53 mutation. Tumor mutational burden was low at 4 mutations/Mb; microsatellite stable.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion and complete pre-treatment dental evaluation, she underwent percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube placement given a mild-to-moderate baseline oropharyngeal dysphagia documented by speech-language pathology. She subsequently received definitive concurrent chemoradiation: intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) using a simultaneous integrated boost technique to 70 Gy in 35 fractions to the high-risk PTV, with concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 on days 1, 22, and 43. She completed all 35 radiation fractions without interruption. However, she experienced significant toxicity during the course, including grade 3 oral mucositis requiring a 5-day inpatient hospitalization for IV hydration, IV morphine PCA, and escalation to full PEG tube feedings in week 4. Grade 2 nephrotoxicity developed during week 6 (creatinine rise to 1.4 from baseline 0.8 mg/dL), which required IV hydration and IV magnesium repletion. The third cisplatin cycle was accordingly dose-reduced to 75 mg/m2. She ultimately received 2.75 of 3 planned cisplatin cycles total.

Post-treatment PET-CT performed approximately 10 weeks after the last radiation fraction demonstrated a complete metabolic response at both the primary base of tongue site and the left level IIA lymph node, with resolution of previously avid disease to background. Flexible laryngoscopy at that time showed healing mucosa without gross residual tumor. She was followed at the 3-month mark as well, at which point the PEG tube had been removed approximately 2 weeks prior, full oral intake had been reestablished, she had returned to part-time teaching, and her ECOG performance status had recovered to 1.

She presents today for her 6-month post-treatment surveillance visit. She reports continued and ongoing improvement since her last visit. Swallowing function has further improved and she can now tolerate a regular diet with some modifications — she avoids hard, dry, or very fibrous foods (such as raw vegetables and tough meats) but is otherwise managing well. She denies frank dysphagia to liquids. She continues to experience xerostomia which she rates as mild and bothersome primarily in the morning and during prolonged speaking at work. She is using biotene spray and xylitol-based lozenges, which provide modest symptomatic relief. She explicitly denies any new or recurrent throat pain, odynophagia, otalgia, or referred ear pain. She denies any sense of a lump returning in the throat or in the neck. No trismus or difficulty opening the mouth. No new skin breakdown over the neck. She notes mild, intermittent left neck stiffness, which she attributes to residual tightness from her radiation. She has not yet been referred to physical therapy for this complaint but mentions it has been bothering her slightly more over the past few weeks.

Dietary and nutritional status: her weight has been stable to gradually improving. She is eating three meals a day by mouth and no longer requires any supplemental enteral nutrition. She has been taking oral nutritional supplements (Boost Plus) between meals voluntarily to help with weight recovery. She confirms the PEG tube site has healed completely.

Psychosocially, she reports that she is doing well emotionally. She has connected with other patients through a head and neck cancer survivorship group at the hospital. She continues to feel anxious around surveillance appointments, which she describes as understandable given the nature of the diagnosis, but states her overall anxiety is manageable. **ASSESSMENT AND PLAN**

**1. HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, base of tongue, T2N1M0 — currently with no evidence of disease, 6 months post-chemoradiation**

Ms. Okonkwo continues to demonstrate an excellent treatment response with durable complete remission at the 6-month surveillance mark. The combination of a clear CT scan of the neck and chest today, a normal flexible laryngoscopy, and the absence of any clinical exam findings suggestive of recurrence is very reassuring. We are now 6 months out from the completion of her 70 Gy IMRT with concurrent cisplatin and the trajectory looks favorable.

I reviewed with her that HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer, particularly in never-smokers with T2N1 disease and a complete metabolic response after definitive chemoradiation, carries an excellent long-term outlook. Data from multiple institutions suggest 3-year locoregional control rates above 90% and 5-year disease-specific survival exceeding 85% for this favorable biology group. Most recurrences in HPV-positive disease, when they do occur, happen within the first 2 years — so continued vigilance remains important, but the 6-month timepoint with a clean scan is an important milestone.

I discussed with Ms. Okonkwo that per emerging evidence (including retrospective data on HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer surveillance patterns and data from centers like MD Anderson and MSKCC), there is no proven benefit to continued PET-CT surveillance in patients who have achieved a complete metabolic response and are now beyond 6 months. Moving forward, we will transition to clinical surveillance with CT scanning of the neck (and chest as indicated) rather than PET-CT, at 6-month intervals during years 2 and 3, and then annually thereafter. PET-CT will be reserved for cases of clinical suspicion or equivocal CT findings.

Plan: Next imaging surveillance scheduled for 12 months post-chemoradiation (approximately 6 months from now): CT neck with contrast. We will add chest CT at that visit given the 1-year mark. Continue quarterly clinical examinations with flexible laryngoscopy in the interim.

**2. Late effects of chemoradiation — xerostomia and dysphagia**

Grade 1 xerostomia persists, as expected given the radiation doses received to the bilateral parotid glands (mean left parotid dose 24 Gy, mean right parotid 22 Gy). While these were kept below the 26 Gy mean dose constraint to limit xerostomia risk, some salivary dysfunction is expected and may continue to improve gradually over the next 12–24 months. She is currently managing well with biotene spray and xylitol lozenges. I discussed the potential benefit of pilocarpine 5 mg TID as an oral sialagogue — patient is interested in trying this. Will initiate pilocarpine 5 mg orally three times daily with meals. Counseled on potential cholinergic side effects (sweating, flushing, increased urinary frequency) and to take with food to minimize GI side effects. If tolerating well and beneficial, may continue long-term.

Mild dysphagia to certain solid textures is expected given radiation to the base of tongue and oropharynx and the patient's mild pre-existing dysphagia. She is not aspirating (per prior modified barium swallow study at 3 months). A repeat swallowing evaluation will be deferred for now unless she develops new symptoms such as choking, aspiration events, or worsening dysphagia. Speech and swallowing therapy had been ongoing at the 3-month visit — I encouraged her to continue if sessions are still active and to practice home exercises (Shaker exercises, effortful swallowing, Mendelsohn maneuver) to maintain pharyngeal muscle strength.

**3. Left neck stiffness — possible early radiation-related fibrosis**

Her complaint of progressive left neck stiffness is consistent with radiation-induced fibrosis of the neck soft tissues, which can develop 6–12 months post-treatment and may continue to evolve for up to 2 years. On exam, there is mild soft tissue induration in the left lower neck but no mass and no adenopathy. This is a recognized late effect of radiation to the neck. Referral to physical therapy placed today for cervical range-of-motion exercises, myofascial release, and stretching. Also discussed with the patient the importance of home neck range-of-motion exercises daily, particularly cervical flexion, extension, lateral flexion, and rotation. She was provided with a handout. Will monitor at subsequent visits; if fibrosis progresses significantly, we may consider referral for pentoxifylline/vitamin E therapy, though evidence is mixed.

**4. Hypothyroidism — worsening, consistent with radiation-induced thyroid injury**

TSH today is 6.8 mIU/L with a low-normal free T4 of 0.9 ng/dL, representing a meaningful change from the normal TSH recorded at the 3-month surveillance visit. This is consistent with the well-recognized phenomenon of radiation-induced hypothyroidism, which can occur months to years after neck irradiation and affects roughly 30–50% of patients treated for head and neck cancer with radiation fields encompassing the thyroid gland. The patient's prior history of levothyroxine-requiring hypothyroidism (pre-existing, likely Hashimoto thyroiditis) likely makes her more susceptible.

I communicated with her primary care physician via a portal message today. The patient's levothyroxine was most recently at 75 mcg daily. Given the current TSH and clinical context, I am recommending an increase to 88 mcg daily (standard step-up dose). She will have repeat TSH and free T4 checked by her PCP in 6 weeks to confirm normalization. Ongoing thyroid function monitoring should be performed at least semi-annually given the ongoing radiation effect on the thyroid gland.

**5. Renal function — monitoring**

Creatinine has returned to 1.0 mg/dL, near her baseline of 0.8 mg/dL, with complete recovery from the grade 2 cisplatin-related nephrotoxicity experienced during treatment. GFR is estimated at >60 mL/min. Ongoing monitoring with annual CMP is appropriate. No nephrology referral needed at this time.

**6. Hemoglobin — mild anemia**

Hemoglobin of 12.9 g/dL is mildly below normal for a premenopausal woman (normal lower limit ~12.0) but improving; this is consistent with ongoing recovery from treatment-related effects and improved nutritional status. Iron studies and B12/folate will be ordered to rule out a nutritional deficiency component given prior prolonged PEG dependence and reduced oral intake. Will review results at next visit. No symptoms of anemia reported.

**7. Nutritional status and weight recovery**

She is encouraged to continue high-protein, high-calorie nutritional choices and to work with our registered dietitian if she desires additional guidance. Nutritional referral offered; patient declines at this time, preferring to manage independently.

**8. Dental/oral health surveillance**

Patient confirms she is compliant with daily 1.1% neutral sodium fluoride tray application, prescribed by dental oncology, for prevention of radiation-induced caries. She has a dental oncology appointment scheduled in the next month. Continued vigilance for osteoradionecrosis of the mandible is appropriate given the radiation exposure; she is instructed to avoid dental extractions without oncologic dentistry consultation, to report any jaw pain, swelling, or non-healing wounds promptly.

**9. Psychosocial / survivorship**

Ms. Okonkwo is engaged with the HPV-positive head and neck cancer support group and appears to be coping well emotionally. She has excellent family support. We discussed the HPV transmission aspects of oropharyngeal cancer in the context of her diagnosis; she indicated her treating team had previously provided information about this and she did not have additional questions today. We reviewed the importance of HPV vaccination for eligible household members and younger family members. Survivorship care plan updated today and provided to the patient in writing, summarizing her diagnosis, treatment received, long-term follow-up schedule, and surveillance plan. She was encouraged to bring her primary care physician into the loop for co-management of late effects.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the neck was performed from the skull base through the thoracic inlet with extension through the upper chest, following the intravenous administration of 100 mL of iodinated contrast material (iohexol 350 mg I/mL) with arterial and venous phase acquisitions. Images were reconstructed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes using a soft tissue kernel at 1.5 mm slice thickness. The study is compared with the 12-month post-treatment surveillance CT neck from approximately 6 months prior, the 6-month post-treatment CT from approximately 1 year prior, and the pre-treatment staging CT neck and PET-CT.

FINDINGS:

Primary Site - Left Base of Tongue and Oropharynx:
The left base of tongue demonstrates expected post-treatment architectural change. There is smooth, regular soft tissue contour at the primary site without a discrete mass, nodular density, or focal area of pathologic enhancement. The previously described primary lesion measuring 2.2 cm, which had demonstrated intense FDG avidity on staging PET-CT, shows no imaging correlate to suggest viable tumor. There is mild asymmetric volume loss in the left base of tongue musculature, reflecting post-radiation fibrotic change, which is unchanged in extent and character compared with the prior 12-month examination. The left vallecula is patent and without soft tissue infiltration. No abnormal mucosal thickening along the posterior pharyngeal wall, posterior tongue base, or contiguous lingual tonsil. The fat planes in the parapharyngeal and retropharyngeal regions are preserved bilaterally.

Left neck: The previously documented left level IIA lymph node, which measured 2.7 cm with central necrosis at initial diagnosis and demonstrated marked interval decrease on all prior surveillance studies, now appears as a small ovoid fibrous-density structure measuring approximately 7 x 5 mm in short axis. This nodal remnant is again stable compared to the 12-month imaging and continues to demonstrate no suspicious internal architecture, no focal necrosis, no abnormal contrast enhancement, and no increase in size. Appearance remains consistent with post-treatment fibrotic nodal residuum. No additional enlarged or morphologically suspicious left-sided lymph nodes are identified at levels I through V. The left retropharyngeal nodal region is clear.

Right neck: No cervical lymphadenopathy. No enlarged lymph nodes along the right levels I through V. The right retropharyngeal nodal region is unremarkable.

Bilateral parotid glands demonstrate expected post-radiation parenchymal volume loss and fatty infiltration, left slightly more than right, consistent with radiation-induced change following IMRT with parotid-sparing technique. No focal parotid masses or ductal dilation. The left submandibular gland shows heterogeneous parenchymal texture with mildly reduced volume and increased fibrous-density stroma in the lateral soft tissue bed, consistent with late radiation effect, unchanged compared to prior. The right submandibular gland appears intact without suspicious focal lesion. The sublingual spaces are symmetric and without mass.

The thyroid gland is symmetric in size and of homogeneous parenchymal attenuation bilaterally. No focal nodule, cyst, or dominant mass is identified. The gland appears somewhat decreased in volume compared to the pre-treatment baseline study, which may reflect mild radiation-induced parenchymal atrophy, consistent with the clinical history of post-radiation hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine supplementation. Hyoid Bone and Larynx:
The hyoid bone is intact. The supraglottic, glottic, and subglottic larynx is within normal limits. The arytenoids are symmetrically positioned. The true and false vocal cords appear symmetric and normally positioned. The pyriform sinuses are clear bilaterally. No laryngeal mass or cartilage erosion.

Oropharynx and Adjacent Spaces:
The soft palate and uvula are within normal limits. The tonsils are not enlarged. The nasopharynx is unremarkable; no suspicious soft tissue fullness along the posterior or lateral walls. The parapharyngeal, masticator, and buccal spaces are all intact.

Skeletal Structures:
The cervical spine demonstrates mild multilevel degenerative disc disease without significant spinal canal or neural foraminal narrowing. The mandible shows no osteonecrosis; there are no areas of cortical disruption, moth-eaten lucency, or periosteal reaction. The skull base is intact without destructive lesion. The bony calvaria is unremarkable on the included images.

Soft Tissues of the Neck:
There is mild soft tissue induration and subcutaneous thickening along the left lateral neck, most pronounced at the level IIA/IIB junction, consistent with the clinically known radiation-induced fibrosis. This has a stable, non-progressive appearance compared with prior imaging. No inflammatory phlegmon, abscess collection, or fistulous tract identified.

Limited Upper Chest Survey:
The lung apices and upper lobes are included on this examination. No focal pulmonary parenchymal consolidation, nodule, or mass is identified in the visualized portions of the lungs. No pleural effusion. The mediastinal contour is unremarkable in the included field. No pathologic mediastinal or bilateral hilar adenopathy. The visualized osseous structures of the thorax are without suspicious lytic or blastic lesion.

IMPRESSION:

1. No imaging evidence of locoregional recurrence at the primary left base of tongue site. Post-radiation architectural changes are present but stable and without features to suggest active tumor at this time.

2. The previously involved left level IIA lymph node continues to regress, now measuring approximately 7 x 5 mm in short axis - a small fibrotic nodal remnant without morphologic features to suggest recurrent or viable nodal disease. This represents continued interval improvement from the 2.7 cm necrotic node at initial diagnosis.

3. No new suspicious cervical or upper mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

6. No pulmonary nodule or mediastinal mass identified on limited upper chest survey.

7. Thyroid gland without discrete nodule; mild volume reduction consistent with radiation effect and clinical hypothyroidism.

Overall, these findings are reassuring and consistent with durable locoregional control at the 18-month post-treatment surveillance timepoint. The findings are essentially unchanged from the 12-month study, supporting ongoing surveillance without further intervention based on imaging alone.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Margaret Okonkwo is a 53-year-old Nigerian-American female, never-smoker, returning today for her 12-month post-treatment surveillance visit following definitive concurrent chemoradiation for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue. HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo was diagnosed approximately 12 months ago with HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, staged T2N1M0 (Stage I per AJCC 8th edition, HPV-positive oropharynx classification), following evaluation of a 2.5 cm left level II cervical lymph node and a 2.2 cm left base of tongue mass identified on CT neck and confirmed on PET-CT staging. Pathology confirmed moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with strong diffuse p16 positivity and positive high-risk HPV RNA in situ hybridization. Fine needle aspiration of the left level IIA node confirmed metastatic squamous carcinoma. NGS from the primary biopsy specimen demonstrated a PIK3CA E545K hotspot mutation, TRAF3 truncating mutation, EP300 and FBXW7 mutations, and KMT2D truncating mutation, with low tumor mutational burden (4 mut/Mb) and microsatellite stability. PD-L1 CPS was reported at 18 by external send-out.

She was evaluated at our multidisciplinary tumor board and treated with definitive concurrent chemoradiation: IMRT with simultaneous integrated boost to 70 Gy in 35 fractions to the high-risk planning target volume, with elective nodal coverage per protocol. Cisplatin was administered at 100 mg/m2 on days 1 and 22; the third planned cycle on day 43 was dose-reduced to 75 mg/m2 due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity and concurrent grade 3 mucositis. She required hospitalization during week 4 of treatment for grade 3 oral mucositis with severe pain, complete inability to tolerate oral intake, and associated weight loss of 5 kg. A prophylactic PEG tube had been placed prior to the start of chemoradiation and was used for full nutritional support during the acute toxicity period.

She completed all 35 fractions of radiation without treatment breaks. Post-treatment PET-CT at approximately 10 weeks showed complete metabolic response at the primary site and at the involved left level IIA lymph node, with no new sites of disease. The PEG tube was removed at approximately 3 months post-treatment after successful transition to full oral intake.

At her 6-month surveillance visit and accompanying CT neck/chest, she continued to show no evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease, with the left level IIA nodal region showing a small fibrotic remnant and no suspicious enhancement. She was noted at that visit to have grade 2 late radiation fibrosis of the left neck with reduced range of motion, and radiation-induced hypothyroidism with a TSH of 7.2, necessitating an upward adjustment in her levothyroxine dose from 75 mcg to 100 mcg daily. Physical therapy for cervical myofascial release and range-of-motion exercises was initiated.

She presents today for her 12-month comprehensive annual assessment. Since the last visit, she reports ongoing improvement in swallowing function, continued mild residual xerostomia, and improving neck range of motion with physical therapy. She remains employed full-time as a high school English teacher. She denies any new throat pain, odynophagia, hemoptysis, hoarseness, new neck masses, otalgia, or unintentional weight loss.
