1. HPV-POSITIVE OROPHARYNGEAL SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, LEFT BASE OF TONGUE, T2N1M0 — NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE AT 12 MONTHS POST-TREATMENT

Ms. Okonkwo has reached the 12-month post-treatment milestone with no clinical, endoscopic, or radiographic evidence of recurrent or residual disease. The current CT neck/chest shows no concerning findings, and her exam with flexible laryngoscopy is entirely reassuring. She achieved a complete metabolic response on her 10-week post-treatment PET-CT, and this durable response has been maintained on all subsequent imaging and clinical assessments.

Her tumor biology is highly favorable: HPV-positive, p16-positive, never-smoker, T2N1, complete metabolic response after definitive chemoradiation. Published series from RTOG 0129, TROG 12.01, and ORATOR trial cohorts consistently demonstrate that patients with her profile have 5-year disease-specific survival rates exceeding 85-90%. We discussed these data with the patient today in plain language, emphasizing that reaching 12 months without recurrence represents a meaningful prognostic milestone and that with each additional clear surveillance visit, her overall risk of relapse continues to decline.

Surveillance plan: We will continue clinical surveillance at 6-month intervals through year 3, then annually thereafter per current head and neck oncology guidelines. CT neck and chest will be obtained at 18 months post-treatment (planned). We do not intend to obtain PET-CT at this time given the complete and durable response, lack of any clinical concern for recurrence, and existing data that routine late PET-CT in complete responders carries a low diagnostic yield and potential for false positives. She should return to the emergency department or contact the clinic immediately if she develops a new neck mass, rapidly progressive dysphagia, hemoptysis, or other concerning symptoms between visits.

2. LATE RADIATION EFFECTS — XEROSTOMIA AND PHARYNGEAL DYSMOTILITY

Xerostomia is currently grade 1, tolerable, and managed with Biotene spray, xylitol lozenges, and generous hydration. She should continue these measures. There is no evidence that pilocarpine would add meaningful benefit at this stage given her current symptom burden; we will revisit if symptoms worsen. She was counseled to avoid medications with anticholinergic side effects and to maintain meticulous oral hydration.

Swallowing function has continued to improve and her MBS shows only mild residual dysmotility without aspiration. She will continue twice-weekly swallowing therapy with our SLP team with a structured home exercise program including Shaker exercises, Mendelsohn maneuver practice, and effortful swallow. Dietary modification (avoiding hard, dry, crumbly foods) is appropriate and does not significantly limit her quality of life. We do not anticipate need for PEG tube reinsertion based on her current trajectory.

Dental surveillance is ongoing with dental oncology. Daily prescription fluoride is essential given radiation dose to the mandible and salivary glands. She is doing well with this.

3. RADIATION-RELATED LEFT NECK FIBROSIS

Grade 2 late radiation fibrosis of the left neck noted at 8 months is improving with physical therapy. She reports significant improvement in range of motion and decreased stiffness. She will continue her physical therapy regimen and home stretching program. We will reassess at the next visit. No evidence of radiation-induced vascular injury; carotid duplex screening per institutional protocol has not yet reached the indicated timeframe (typically starting at 5 years post-radiation).

4. RADIATION-INDUCED HYPOTHYROIDISM

TSH is now 2.1 on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily, within the normal reference range. Thyroid function is well-controlled at this time. She will continue current dose with annual thyroid function monitoring. She was counseled on the expected natural history of radiation-induced hypothyroidism (often progressive) and the importance of continued monitoring. We will alert her primary care physician to continue annual TSH testing going forward.

5. RENAL FUNCTION — RESOLVED CISPLATIN NEPHROTOXICITY

Creatinine is 0.9 mg/dL, essentially at her pre-treatment baseline of 0.8 mg/dL. Renal function has fully recovered from the transient grade 2 nephrotoxicity during cisplatin therapy. Magnesium is now normal without supplementation. No specific renal monitoring beyond routine labs is needed at this time.

6. SURVIVORSHIP AND PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT

The patient expressed a strong interest in connecting with a survivorship program and HPV-positive head and neck cancer peer support group. We will provide referrals to our institutional survivorship clinic and to external resources including the Head and Neck Cancer Alliance and SPOHNC (Support for People with Oral and Head and Neck Cancer). A formal survivorship care plan summarizing her diagnosis, treatment, completed monitoring, and ongoing needs will be generated by our survivorship nurse coordinator and provided to her and her primary care physician. We discussed the importance of continued sun-protection, avoidance of any tobacco exposure, continued dental vigilance, and routine health maintenance.

We also addressed her health anxiety directly. Ongoing worry about recurrence is common and normal in head and neck cancer survivors, and we validated her experience. We recommended continued engagement with mind-body therapies such as yoga and mindfulness, which she has already incorporated into her routine. Formal psychological support or a referral to our oncology psychosocial support team is available if her anxiety worsens.

7. RETURN TO CARE

Ms. Okonkwo will return in approximately 6 months for her 18-month surveillance visit, at which time CT neck with contrast will be repeated per our protocol. She was provided with the oncology nurse navigator contact information for any interim concerns. Labs to be repeated at next visit include CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, and TSH.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

To briefly review her history: she initially presented approximately two years ago with left-sided throat discomfort, globus sensation, and left-sided otalgia. Workup at that time revealed a 2.2 cm left base of tongue mass with a left level IIA necrotic lymph node (2.7 cm). Biopsy of the base of tongue confirmed moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma with strong, diffuse p16 positivity and HPV RNA ISH positivity. Fine needle aspiration of the left level IIA node confirmed metastatic squamous cell carcinoma. Staging PET-CT confirmed locoregional disease only -- intensely hypermetabolic primary (SUVmax 14.2) and hypermetabolic left IIA node (SUVmax 11.8) with no distant disease. Tumor board consensus favored definitive concurrent chemoradiation; she was not eligible for available de-escalation trials due to the necrotic lymphadenopathy exclusion criterion.

She completed definitive IMRT (70 Gy to the high-risk planning target volume in 35 fractions via SIB-VMAT technique) with concurrent cisplatin 100 mg/m2 on days 1 and 22, and a dose-reduced third cycle at 75 mg/m2 on day 43 due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity and grade 3 mucositis. She required hospitalization during week 4 of treatment for confluent grade 3 oropharyngeal mucositis with complete inability to tolerate oral intake, necessitating IV hydration, IV PCA morphine, and escalation to full enteral nutrition through her prophylactic PEG tube. She also experienced grade 2 radiation dermatitis, mild oral candidiasis, hypomagnesemia requiring repletion, and notable fatigue. She completed all 35 fractions without treatment interruption.

Flexible laryngoscopy at that time was reassuring with healing mucosa and no gross residual tumor. Her PEG tube was removed at approximately 3 months post-treatment once she had re-established adequate oral intake.

Subsequent surveillance CT of the neck and chest at 6 months and 12 months post-treatment both showed no evidence of local recurrence or distant metastatic disease. At the 12-month visit, modified barium swallow confirmed mild pharyngeal dysmotility without aspiration. She was also noted at the 8-month mark to have developed grade 2 late radiation-related cervical fibrosis treated with physical therapy, and radiation-induced hypothyroidism requiring an increase in her levothyroxine dose from 75 mcg to 100 mcg daily. Most recent 18-month surveillance CT of the neck (obtained in advance of today's visit) showed no evidence of recurrence, with a stable, small fibrotic remnant in the left IIA nodal region.

1. HPV-POSITIVE OROPHARYNGEAL SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, LEFT BASE OF TONGUE, T2N1M0 -- 18-MONTH POST-TREATMENT SURVEILLANCE, NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE

Ms. Okonkwo continues to demonstrate an excellent and durable response to treatment. At 18 months following completion of definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT, she has no clinical or radiographic evidence of local, regional, or distant recurrence. The primary base of tongue site appears entirely healed on endoscopy. The previously involved left IIA node has regressed dramatically from 2.7 cm to a small non-suspicious fibrotic remnant on serial imaging. This trajectory is entirely consistent with what we expect in HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer with an excellent initial complete metabolic response, particularly in a never-smoking patient.

We reviewed with Ms. Okonkwo the current literature landscape around surveillance in this setting. We noted that per emerging data, including the pooled analysis from several cooperative group trials and retrospective institutional series, the recurrence risk for patients with HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer after achieving complete response continues to decrease with time. The vast majority of recurrences, when they do occur, happen within the first 2 years. At 18 months post-treatment without any evidence of recurrence, her likelihood of achieving durable cure is very high. We discussed the current ASCO head and neck cancer surveillance guidelines, which support clinical examination as the primary tool, with imaging used selectively based on clinical suspicion rather than on a rigid fixed schedule for patients with a documented complete metabolic response.

We also briefly reviewed the de-escalation trial literature at this visit given her ongoing interest -- including ORATOR2, NRG-HN002, and the PATHOS trial framework. While these are relevant to newly diagnosed patients being considered for treatment intensity reduction, they inform our understanding of the favorable biology in HPV-positive, never-smoking patients like Ms. Okonkwo, reinforcing that she received appropriate standard-of-care treatment and has every reason to expect long-term disease-free survival.

CT NECK WITH CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 52-year-old female with 6-week history of progressive left-sided throat discomfort, odynophagia, referred otalgia, and a firm non-tender left level II cervical lymph node measuring approximately 2.5 cm on examination. Persistent symptoms despite a 10-day course of amoxicillin-clavulanate. Evaluate for underlying mass or lymphadenopathy. History of hypothyroidism and GERD. No prior head and neck imaging on file for comparison.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the neck was performed from the skull base through the thoracic inlet using a helical acquisition technique before and after intravenous administration of iohexol contrast (100 mL at 3 mL/sec via right antecubital vein). Images were acquired in the axial plane at 0.625 mm slice thickness and reconstructed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes with soft tissue and bone algorithm kernels. No adverse reaction to contrast. Limited chest windows were obtained through the lung apices and upper mediastinum.

FINDINGS:

Primary Site - Base of Tongue / Oropharynx:
There is an ill-defined but discernible soft tissue mass measuring approximately 2.2 cm in greatest dimension (AP x transverse x craniocaudal: 2.2 x 1.9 x 2.0 cm) centered at the left base of tongue. The mass demonstrates avid and heterogeneous enhancement following contrast administration, with slightly irregular margins. It arises from the left glossotonsillar sulcus region and extends anteriorly and inferiorly toward the left vallecula. The epiglottis is displaced but appears intact without definite direct invasion. There is asymmetric fullness and thickening of the left base of tongue mucosa relative to the right side. The lingual tonsil tissue on the right appears normal. The oropharyngeal airway is not significantly compromised. Pre-epiglottic space appears preserved. No definite involvement of the posterior pharyngeal wall or piriform sinus.

Cervical Lymph Nodes:
There are several enlarged lymph nodes in the left cervical chain at levels II and IIA with imaging features highly concerning for metastatic involvement. The dominant node in the left level IIA region measures 2.7 cm in short axis diameter and demonstrates peripheral rim enhancement with a large central area of low density consistent with central liquefactive necrosis. This degree of central breakdown in the absence of prior treatment is a well-established morphologic feature of metastatic squamous cell carcinoma and is not expected in reactive or infectious adenopathy. Surrounding the dominant necrotic node, there are two to three additional mildly enlarged but non-necrotic nodes in the left level II region, the largest measuring approximately 1.3 cm in short axis diameter, with borderline preservation of the fatty hilum. There are no matted or confluent nodal conglomerates. No frank extranodal soft tissue extension is appreciated on the current study, though the dominant necrotic node abuts the posterior belly of the left digastric muscle with loss of the intervening fat plane over a short segment, which may warrant further characterization.

The right cervical chain levels I through VI demonstrates no enlarged, morphologically suspicious, or necrotic lymph nodes. The bilateral retropharyngeal nodal stations appear normal. The bilateral posterior cervical triangles (level V) are clear. Left level IB and level III nodes are within normal size limits with no internal necrosis.

Salivary Glands:
The bilateral parotid glands are symmetric, homogeneous, and without focal mass lesion, ductal dilatation, or calculi. The submandibular glands are symmetric and unremarkable bilaterally. No sialolithiasis. No intraductal obstruction.

Thyroid Gland:
The thyroid gland is normal in size and morphology. There is a subcentimeter (5 mm) hypodense nodule within the right thyroid lobe, appearing simple and without suspicious features (no microcalcifications, no hypervascularity). This likely represents a small colloid cyst or benign thyroid nodule. No dominant thyroid mass. No thyroid enlargement or goiter. No suspicious cervical thyroid tissue.

Larynx and Hypopharynx:
The laryngeal architecture is preserved. The true and false vocal cords appear symmetric and normally positioned. The arytenoids and aryepiglottic folds are symmetric. Piriform sinuses are symmetric and patent. No subglottic extension or cricoid involvement. No pre-epiglottic or paraglottic fat infiltration.

Vascular Structures:
The bilateral common, internal, and external carotid arteries are patent and demonstrate normal caliber and enhancement. No carotid encasement or displacement by the dominant necrotic lymph node, though medial nodal position places the node in proximity to the left internal jugular vein, which appears patent. No venous thrombosis.

Cervical Soft Tissues and Bones:
The strap musculature and platysma are symmetric. No prevertebral soft tissue thickening. The visualized cervical vertebral bodies demonstrate no aggressive osseous lesions. No fracture or malalignment.

Chest Windows (Limited Survey through Upper Thorax):
The lung apices and upper mediastinum are included on the acquisition. No pulmonary nodules or masses are identified in the visualized lung parenchyma. No hilar or mediastinal adenopathy. No pleural effusion. Osseous structures of the upper thorax appear unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:

1. Enhancing soft tissue mass measuring approximately 2.2 cm at the left base of tongue, extending toward the left vallecula, with morphologic features highly suspicious for a primary malignant neoplasm. In the clinical context of a 52-year-old with odynophagia, left neck mass, and referred otalgia, squamous cell carcinoma of the oropharynx is the foremost diagnostic consideration. Direct tissue sampling is strongly warranted.

2. Dominant left level IIA lymph node measuring 2.7 cm with extensive central necrosis and peripheral rim enhancement, accompanied by additional mildly enlarged left level II lymph nodes. This constellation is strongly suggestive of metastatic cervical lymphadenopathy, most consistent with regional nodal spread from an oropharyngeal primary. Possible minimal loss of fat plane adjacent to the left digastric muscle is noted but extranodal extension cannot be definitively confirmed or excluded on this study.

3. No contralateral cervical lymphadenopathy. No retropharyngeal or posterior triangle nodal involvement.

4. No evidence of distant metastatic disease on the limited chest survey included. Dedicated chest and staging imaging with PET-CT would be appropriate following tissue confirmation.

5. Small right thyroid nodule (5 mm), likely benign. May be noted at follow-up per thyroid nodule guidelines, low clinical priority in this setting.

Communicated to referring otolaryngology service.

SPECIMEN SUBMITTED:

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GROSS DESCRIPTION:

Specimen A: Received fresh in a labeled container, "left base of tongue biopsy." The container holds multiple soft tissue fragments, pale tan-white to pale pink, ranging in greatest dimension from 0.2 cm to 0.6 cm, with an aggregate greatest dimension of approximately 1.2 cm. The fragments are irregular, somewhat ragged in contour, and show a firm to rubbery consistency. All fragments are entirely submitted for processing in one cassette, labeled A1.

Representative sections submitted per standard protocol. Sections stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). Immunohistochemical studies performed as noted below.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Histologic sections of the left base of tongue biopsy reveal fragments of squamous epithelium with underlying lamina propria and lymphoid stroma. Within the submitted material, there is an infiltrative neoplasm composed of nests, cords, and irregular sheets of polygonal epithelioid cells with moderately enlarged nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and moderate amounts of pale eosinophilic to amphophilic cytoplasm. Nuclear pleomorphism is moderate. Mitotic figures are readily identified, including atypical forms. A background lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate is present.

The tumor demonstrates a non-keratinizing to minimally keratinizing morphology, with absence of extensive individual cell keratinization or surface keratin pearl formation. There is no prominent intercellular bridge formation or overt surface dysplasia, features consistent with a tonsillar/oropharyngeal origin. The carcinoma shows infiltrative growth with percolation through the lymphoid stroma. There is no definitive perineural invasion or lymphovascular invasion identified within the limited biopsy material, though assessment is inherently limited.

The overlying surface squamous epithelium, where present, appears intact without evidence of surface dysplasia or carcinoma in situ.

Overall, the morphologic features are consistent with a moderately differentiated, non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma, histologically compatible with an oropharyngeal / base of tongue primary site.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
ANCILLARY STUDIES:

IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY (performed on Specimen A, block A1):

  p16 (INK4a, CINtec clone E6H4, Roche/Ventana): POSITIVE -- Strong, diffuse nuclear AND cytoplasmic staining in >70% of tumor cells, in a block-positive pattern. This result meets the established threshold for surrogate HPV-associated status in oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma (defined as >=70% nuclear and cytoplasmic staining of at least 2+ intensity).

  CK5/6 (D5/16B4): Positive; strong membranous and cytoplasmic staining in tumor cells, confirming squamous lineage.

  p40 (BC28): Positive; nuclear staining confirming squamous differentiation.

  Ki-67 (MIB-1): High proliferative index, estimated 60-70% of tumor cell nuclei positive.

IN SITU HYBRIDIZATION:

  HPV RNA ISH, high-risk subtypes (RNAscope HPV-HR probe, includes subtypes 16, 18, 31, 33, 35, 45, 51, 52, 56, 58; Advanced Cell Diagnostics): POSITIVE -- Punctate (nuclear dot) pattern of staining identified in tumor cell nuclei, consistent with episomal and/or integrated high-risk HPV RNA expression. Positive and negative controls performed satisfactorily.

Note: HPV genotyping by PCR or subtype-specific ISH is NOT routinely performed on initial biopsy and is not included in this report. Further molecular characterization may be performed at the discretion of the treating team.

Note: EBV-encoded small RNA ISH (EBER) was NOT performed, as the histomorphology and clinical context are not suggestive of an EBV-related neoplasm. If clinically indicated, this can be performed on request.

ADDITIONAL STUDIES PENDING (to be reported as addendum if ordered):
  - PD-L1 immunohistochemistry (22C3 pharmDx or equivalent) -- ORDERED, result pending per oncology request. Will be reported as addendum upon completion.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DIAGNOSIS:

A. LEFT BASE OF TONGUE, DIRECTED BIOPSY:

   SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, MODERATELY DIFFERENTIATED, NON-KERATINIZING TYPE.

   -- p16 immunohistochemistry: STRONGLY POSITIVE (block-positive pattern, >70% cells), consistent with HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
   -- High-risk HPV RNA ISH: POSITIVE (punctate nuclear pattern).
   -- Morphology: Non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma; keratinizing morphology absent.
   -- See comment.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
COMMENT:

The combination of non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma morphology, strong diffuse p16 block-positive immunoreactivity (meeting ASCO/CAP criteria), and positive high-risk HPV RNA in situ hybridization is diagnostic of HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. This finding is highly concordant with the clinical and radiologic presentation of a left base of tongue primary with ipsilateral cervical lymphadenopathy in a 52-year-old female never-smoker.

Per current CAP/ASCO/AAO-HNS guidelines, the combination of p16 IHC (block-positive) AND confirmatory HPV RNA ISH positivity provides definitive HPV-associated status classification for oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. No further HPV-specific testing is required for staging or prognostic purposes; however, genotyping (likely HPV-16 in this epidemiologic context) may be pursued at the treating physician's discretion.

Differential diagnostic considerations including nasopharyngeal carcinoma (EBV-associated) and metastatic carcinoma from a non-oropharyngeal primary site are considered less likely given the site of biopsy, morphologic features, and HPV/p16 positivity.

Correlation with clinical, endoscopic, and imaging findings is recommended. Formal staging per AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th edition (HPV-mediated oropharyngeal SCC) is advised.

WHOLE-BODY FDG PET/CT SCAN WITH DIAGNOSTIC CT OF NECK, CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS

PATIENT: Margaret Okonkwo
INDICATION: 52-year-old female with newly diagnosed HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, pathologically confirmed by directed biopsy (moderately differentiated SCC, p16 strongly positive by IHC, high-risk HPV RNA ISH positive). Referred for whole-body FDG PET/CT for complete locoregional and systemic staging prior to initiation of definitive therapy. Clinical staging prior to this examination: T2 primary based on CT neck (2.2 cm left base of tongue mass), left cervical level IIA lymphadenopathy with central necrosis.

REFERRING PROVIDER: Head and Neck Oncology

TECHNIQUE: Whole-body FDG PET/CT was performed in accordance with departmental and SNMMI guidelines for oncologic FDG imaging. The patient fasted for a minimum of 6 hours prior to intravenous administration of 10.2 mCi (377 MBq) of F-18 fluorodeoxyglucose. Blood glucose at the time of injection was 89 mg/dL. Following a 60-minute uptake period in a quiet, low-light room with the patient at rest, emission data were acquired from skull base to mid-thigh in 3D mode. Concomitant low-dose CT for attenuation correction and anatomic localization was performed. Additionally, a separate diagnostic-quality CT scan of the neck with IV contrast (Omnipaque 350, 100 mL, injected via antecubital IV) was obtained in the portal venous phase to provide optimal soft tissue characterization of the primary site and cervical lymph node stations. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed with IV contrast for assessment of distant disease. Images were reviewed on dedicated PET/CT workstation with simultaneous display of fused PET/CT, PET-only, and CT-only datasets in all three planes. SUVmax values are reported using lean body mass correction.

COMPARISON: CT neck with contrast (obtained at otolaryngology workup, approximately 5 weeks prior).

---

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK - PRIMARY SITE:
There is an intensely hypermetabolic soft tissue mass involving the left base of tongue with extension toward the left vallecula. On the diagnostic CT component, the lesion measures approximately 2.2 cm in greatest dimension and demonstrates avid heterogeneous enhancement. The deep margin approaches but does not appear to cross the midline. The lesion does not extend to involve the epiglottis, the floor of mouth, or the lingual tonsil on the contralateral side. The overlying mucosa appears intact. FDG uptake within this lesion is markedly elevated, with an SUVmax of 14.2, substantially exceeding background soft tissue and salivary gland activity. The high glycolytic activity is consistent with viable, aggressive squamous cell carcinoma. There is no definitive involvement of the pre-epiglottic fat or the vallecula proper based on the CT component.

The right base of tongue, right tonsil, soft palate, posterior pharyngeal wall, supraglottic larynx, and hypopharynx are unremarkable. No additional FDG-avid mucosal lesion is identified to suggest a synchronous primary oropharyngeal malignancy. The nasopharynx appears normal without evidence of an additional primary.

HEAD AND NECK - LYMPH NODE STATIONS:
Left neck: There is a single dominant, markedly hypermetabolic lymph node in the left level IIA region (jugulodigastric group), measuring approximately 2.6 cm in short-axis diameter. The node demonstrates heterogeneous FDG uptake with an SUVmax of 11.8. On the diagnostic CT component, the nodal architecture is replaced by centrally hypodense, non-enhancing material consistent with necrosis, surrounded by a rim of peripheral enhancement. This pattern is highly characteristic of metastatic squamous cell carcinoma with central necrotic breakdown. A central photopenic region on the PET images corresponds precisely to the CT-defined zone of necrosis. One to two additional smaller lymph nodes in the left level II station measure up to 0.9 cm and demonstrate borderline FDG activity (SUVmax approximately 2.4), which is indeterminate and may reflect reactive adenopathy versus microscopic metastatic involvement; these nodes do not meet size criteria for definitive nodal metastasis. Left levels III, IV, and V are unremarkable. No left level IB, IA, retropharyngeal, or parapharyngeal nodal FDG uptake is identified.

Right neck: No hypermetabolic lymph nodes identified. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes by size criteria. The right jugulodigastric, submandibular, and posterior cervical chain nodes are not FDG-avid.

Bilateral retropharyngeal nodes: No abnormal FDG uptake or CT-evident pathologically enlarged nodes in the retropharyngeal or parapharyngeal spaces bilaterally.

Supraclavicular and infraclavicular regions: No pathologically enlarged or FDG-avid lymph nodes bilaterally.

SALIVARY GLANDS:
The parotid glands are symmetric, with physiologic background FDG uptake. No focal hypermetabolic lesion. The bilateral submandibular glands demonstrate physiologic symmetric mild FDG activity. The sublingual and minor salivary glands are unremarkable.

ORBITS, SKULL BASE, AND CRANIUM:
No hypermetabolic intracranial lesions identified. The calvarium and skull base are intact without evidence of lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions on the CT component. Brain parenchyma is not routinely evaluated on whole-body FDG PET/CT due to high physiologic cerebral glucose metabolism; dedicated brain MRI is recommended if intracranial involvement is clinically suspected.

THYROID GLAND:
The thyroid gland is unremarkable in size and morphology. No focal hypermetabolic thyroid nodule identified. Background thyroid gland FDG uptake is within physiologic range.

CHEST:
LUNGS AND PLEURA: The lungs are clear. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidation identified on the diagnostic CT component. No pleural effusion or pleural thickening. No FDG-avid pulmonary lesion.

MEDIASTINUM: The mediastinal contours are normal. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes are identified. No FDG-avid mediastinal nodal disease. The anterior, middle, and posterior mediastinal compartments are unremarkable.

HILAR REGIONS: Bilateral hila are symmetric and unremarkable. No hilar lymphadenopathy.

HEART AND PERICARDIUM: Cardiac silhouette is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. Physiologic FDG uptake in the myocardium.

BONY THORAX: The visualized ribs, sternum, thoracic spine, and bilateral clavicles demonstrate normal bone density without lytic or blastic lesions. No focal hypermetabolic osseous lesion identified.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:
LIVER: The liver is normal in size and morphology. No focal hepatic lesions on CT. No hepatic FDG uptake beyond physiologic background. No evidence of hepatic metastases.

SPLEEN: Normal in size and homogeneous. No focal FDG-avid lesion.

PANCREAS: Unremarkable. No focal pancreatic lesion.

ADRENAL GLANDS: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology without focal masses or FDG uptake exceeding background.

KIDNEYS: Bilateral kidneys are symmetric with normal parenchymal thickness. Physiologic FDG activity within the collecting systems from urinary excretion. No renal masses or hydronephrosis.

GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT: Normal-caliber loops of small and large bowel. No focal wall thickening or FDG-avid bowel lesion. Physiologic colonic FDG uptake.

PELVIS: The uterus and ovaries are unremarkable. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No pelvic FDG-avid lesion identified.

SKIN AND SOFT TISSUES: No hypermetabolic subcutaneous or soft tissue lesion identified.

IMPRESSION:

1. Intensely hypermetabolic left base of tongue mass (SUVmax 14.2), measuring approximately 2.2 cm, consistent with the known primary squamous cell carcinoma. No CT or PET evidence of extension across the midline or involvement of adjacent structures beyond the base of tongue and vallecula.

2. Single markedly hypermetabolic left level IIA (jugulodigastric) lymph node, 2.6 cm, with central necrosis on CT and corresponding central photopenia on PET (SUVmax 11.8 in the peripheral viable rim), consistent with metastatic nodal involvement with necrosis. This represents the sole site of nodal metastasis identified.

3. One to two smaller left level II nodes with borderline FDG activity are indeterminate; clinical and radiologic correlation is recommended, and these may warrant inclusion in the radiation treatment planning volumes.

5. No FDG-avid or CT-evident distant metastatic disease in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis.

6. No synchronous FDG-avid primary malignancy identified.

Overall, findings are consistent with locoregional disease confined to the primary left base of tongue site and a single left level IIA metastatic lymph node, with no evidence of distant metastatic spread. These findings support a locoregional stage consistent with T2N1 per clinical and radiologic assessment in the context of HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.

SPECIMEN SUBMITTED:
Specimen A: Fine needle aspiration, left level IIA cervical lymph node, ultrasound-guided; direct smears (Papanicolaou-stained and Diff-Quik-stained), liquid-based preparation (ThinPrep), and cell block material.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
CLINICAL HISTORY:
The patient is a 52-year-old never-smoking female of Nigerian-American descent who presents with a recently biopsied left base of tongue mass (prior pathology: squamous cell carcinoma, p16-positive, HPV RNA ISH-positive) and a CT/PET-CT-demonstrated left level IIA cervical lymph node measuring 2.6-2.7 cm with central necrosis (SUVmax 11.8 on PET-CT), concerning for metastatic adenopathy. The procedure is performed for staging confirmation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
PROCEDURE DESCRIPTION:
Ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration of the left level IIA cervical lymph node was performed by the cytopathology service using a 25-gauge needle with 3 passes. Rapid on-site evaluation (ROSE) was performed by the cytopathologist at the time of the procedure, with the preliminary assessment indicating adequate cellular material and suspicious for malignancy. Material was processed for direct smears (fixed and air-dried), liquid-based cytology (ThinPrep), and cell block preparation for ancillary immunohistochemical studies. The procedure was performed without complication under conscious sedation with ultrasound guidance. No immediate adverse events were noted.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
GROSS DESCRIPTION:
Received for rapid on-site evaluation and permanent processing:
- Pass 1: Four direct smears prepared; 2 fixed in 95% ethanol (Papanicolaou stain), 2 air-dried (Diff-Quik stain). Residual needle rinse submitted in CytoLyt fixative for ThinPrep preparation.
- Pass 2: Four direct smears prepared; 2 fixed and 2 air-dried. Residual material submitted in CytoLyt fixative.
- Pass 3: Material submitted entirely into CytoLyt fixative for cell block preparation. The aspirate was frankly cellular, with turbid tan-red fluid noted in the aspirate consistent with a necrotic nodal background.
Cell block: Prepared from combined passes. Following centrifugation, the pellet was fixed in 10% neutral buffered formalin, embedded in paraffin, and sectioned at 5-micron levels. Sections are tan-pink and grossly unremarkable. Multiple levels obtained for H&E staining and immunohistochemical studies.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
DIAGNOSIS:

Specimen A -- LEFT LEVEL IIA CERVICAL LYMPH NODE, ULTRASOUND-GUIDED FINE NEEDLE ASPIRATION:

METASTATIC SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA
- Consistent with oropharyngeal primary (see note)
- p16 (CDKN2A) immunostain: STRONGLY AND DIFFUSELY POSITIVE (see ancillary studies)
- Background necrotic debris present
- No morphologic evidence of keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:
The smears are cellular and consist of cohesive and single atypical epithelial cells set against a background of abundant necrotic debris, lymphocyte-rich inflammatory infiltrate, and occasional macrophages consistent with necrotic nodal tissue. The malignant cells are polygonal to spindled, with moderate to abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, irregular nuclear membranes, coarse chromatin, and prominent nucleoli. Intercellular bridges are focally appreciated on Diff-Quik-stained air-dried smears. Keratinization is not identified; no keratin pearls are seen. The cytomorphology is that of a non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma.

On the ThinPrep preparation, similar malignant epithelial cells are identified in both tight syncytial clusters and as scattered single cells. Atypical mitotic figures are present. No glandular or mucinous differentiation is identified.

The cell block sections show islands and nests of malignant squamous epithelium within a fibrotic and necrotic lymph node background. Lymphoid elements are effaced and largely replaced by tumor deposits. The histomorphology is consistent with metastatic non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma involving a cervical lymph node, with central nodal necrosis.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

IMMUNOHISTOCHEMISTRY (performed on cell block sections):

1. p16 (CINtec, clone E6H4, prediluted; Roche): STRONGLY AND DIFFUSELY POSITIVE

3. CK5/6 (clone D5/16 B4, prediluted): POSITIVE in malignant cells, confirming squamous differentiation

- RESULT: PENDING at time of preliminary report issuance; will be appended upon completion

Note: EBV-encoded RNA (EBER) ISH was not performed at this time. Given the squamous morphology, p40/CK5-6 positivity, and p16 block-positive staining in the clinical setting of a known p16-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, EBV-associated nasopharyngeal carcinoma is not favored; EBER ISH may be added at the discretion of the clinical team or upon amended request.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
NOTE / INTERPRETATION:
The cytomorphology of the aspirate from the left level IIA cervical lymph node is that of metastatic non-keratinizing squamous cell carcinoma. In the clinical context of a confirmed, recently biopsied left base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma (p16-positive, HPV RNA ISH-positive; prior accession [see institutional cross-reference]), this nodal metastasis is most consistent with involvement from the known oropharyngeal primary.

The strongly diffuse block-positive p16 immunostaining pattern (>90% cells, nuclear and cytoplasmic) on the cell block is consistent with transcriptionally-active high-risk HPV-associated carcinoma and correlates directly with p16 positivity documented at the primary site. This finding, in conjunction with the non-keratinizing squamous morphology, is consistent with HPV-driven oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma metastatic to a cervical lymph node.

The presence of extensive background necrosis in the aspirate is consistent with the central necrosis previously identified on CT and PET-CT imaging of this node.

These findings support regional (N1) nodal metastasis from the known primary HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. Correlation with clinical and imaging staging information is recommended.

1. PIK3CA p.E545K — EXON 9 HELICAL DOMAIN HOTSPOT MUTATION
   Gene:                PIK3CA (phosphatidylinositol-4,5-bisphosphate 3-kinase catalytic subunit alpha)
   Variant:             c.1633G>A; p.Glu545Lys
   Variant Type:        Missense, activating
   Variant Allele Freq: 18% (tumor); 0% (germline/normal blood) — confirmed somatic
   Coverage:            Tumor: 814x; Variant reads: 147
   Classification:      Pathogenic — established gain-of-function hotspot
   Chromosomal Context: Co-localizes with focal 3q gain (PIK3CA locus at 3q26.32); possible gene dosage contribution
   Functional Impact:   p.E545K is a well-characterized activating mutation in the helical domain of PIK3CAalpha,
                        resulting in constitutive PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway activation independent of upstream
                        receptor tyrosine kinase signaling. This hotspot is frequently identified in HPV-positive
                        oropharyngeal SCC, occurring in approximately 25-35% of cases. The concomitant focal 3q
                        gain may increase PIK3CA copy number, augmenting oncogenic signaling.
   Clinical Relevance:  POTENTIALLY ACTIONABLE. PI3K pathway inhibitors (e.g., alpelisib, copanlisib) have
                        demonstrated preclinical and early clinical activity in PIK3CA-mutant head and neck SCC.
                        Not currently standard of care in the recurrence-naive setting; relevant if systemic
                        therapy is required for recurrent/metastatic disease. NCCN category 2B consideration.
                        MATCH/TAPUR basket trial eligibility may apply if recurrence occurs.
   Associated Pathways: PI3K-AKT-mTOR signaling

2. TRAF3 p.R118* — LOSS-OF-FUNCTION MUTATION
   Gene:                TRAF3 (TNF receptor-associated factor 3)
   Variant:             c.352C>T; p.Arg118Ter (stop gain)
   Variant Type:        Nonsense, loss-of-function
   Variant Allele Freq: 22% (tumor); 0% (normal) — confirmed somatic
   Coverage:            Tumor: 692x; Variant reads: 152
   Classification:      Likely Pathogenic (tumor suppressor, truncating mutation)
   Functional Impact:   TRAF3 encodes a tumor suppressor involved in innate immune signaling, type I interferon
                        production, and regulation of the non-canonical NF-kappaB pathway. Loss-of-function
                        mutations in TRAF3 lead to constitutive non-canonical NF-kappaB activation, which promotes
                        cell survival, proliferation, and immune evasion. TRAF3 is among the most frequently mutated
                        genes in HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC (reported in ~15-20% of cases), in contrast to its
                        infrequent alteration in HPV-negative HNSCC. Loss of TRAF3 in HPV-associated carcinogenesis
                        may cooperate with viral oncoproteins (E6/E7) to drive malignant transformation.
   Clinical Relevance:  Biologically relevant; not currently directly targetable. May have implications for
                        immune checkpoint inhibitor responsiveness (NF-kappaB pathway activation can modulate
                        tumor immunogenicity). No immediate therapeutic actionability in the curative-intent setting.
   Associated Pathways: NF-kappaB (non-canonical), innate immune signaling, type I IFN pathway

3. KMT2D (MLL2) p.Q3382* — TRUNCATING MUTATION
   Gene:                KMT2D (lysine methyltransferase 2D; also known as MLL4, MLL2)
   Variant:             c.10144C>T; p.Gln3382Ter
   Variant Type:        Nonsense, truncating
   Variant Allele Freq: 19% (tumor); 0% (normal) — confirmed somatic
   Coverage:            Tumor: 728x; Variant reads: 138
   Classification:      Likely Pathogenic (tumor suppressor context)
   Functional Impact:   KMT2D encodes a histone H3K4 methyltransferase that functions as a master regulator of
                        enhancer activation and gene expression. As a member of the COMPASS-like complex, KMT2D
                        establishes H3K4me1 marks at active enhancers, regulating the transcription of tumor
                        suppressor genes and differentiation programs. Truncating mutations in KMT2D are among the
                        most common epigenetic alterations across all HNSCC subtypes (both HPV-positive and
                        HPV-negative), occurring in approximately 15-20% of cases. Loss of KMT2D function leads
                        to widespread enhancer dysregulation and may contribute to dedifferentiation, increased
                        proliferative capacity, and resistance to certain therapeutic modalities.
   Clinical Relevance:  No direct targeted therapy currently approved. May have implications for sensitivity to
                        histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitors or EZH2 inhibitors in synthetic lethality
                        frameworks; this remains investigational. Not directly actionable in standard clinical
                        practice at this time.
   Associated Pathways: Chromatin remodeling, histone modification (H3K4 methylation), enhancer regulation

4. EP300 p.R418C — CHROMATIN REMODELING, VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE
   Gene:                EP300 (E1A binding protein p300; transcriptional coactivator / histone acetyltransferase)
   Variant:             c.1252C>T; p.Arg418Cys
   Variant Type:        Missense
   Variant Allele Freq: 15% (tumor); 0% (normal) — confirmed somatic
   Coverage:            Tumor: 661x; Variant reads: 99
   Classification:      Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS), potentially deleterious
   Functional Impact:   EP300 encodes a transcriptional coactivator with intrinsic histone acetyltransferase (HAT)
                        activity, functioning broadly as a tumor suppressor and transcriptional integrator. p300
                        acetylates histone H3K27 at enhancers and cooperates with CBP in regulating differentiation
                        and apoptosis. The p.R418C substitution occurs within the zinc finger / TAZ1 domain. While
                        EP300 truncating mutations are frequently pathogenic in HNSCC, this missense variant at
                        p.R418C has limited functional characterization to date. Given the tumor suppressor role of
                        EP300 and the missense context affecting a conserved residue, this variant is classified as
                        a VUS with possible functional impact; in silico predictions (SIFT: Deleterious, PolyPhen-2:
                        Probably Damaging, CADD score: 28.4) support potential pathogenicity.
   Clinical Relevance:  Not directly actionable. May co-participate with KMT2D loss in an epigenetic dysregulation
                        landscape common to HNSCC. Continued monitoring of functional significance in the literature
                        is recommended.
   Associated Pathways: Transcriptional coactivation, histone acetyltransferase activity, p53 acetylation

5. FBXW7 p.R505C — UBIQUITIN LIGASE SUBSTRATE ADAPTOR, VUS
   Gene:                FBXW7 (F-box and WD repeat domain containing 7)
   Variant:             c.1513C>T; p.Arg505Cys
   Variant Type:        Missense; occurs at a known mutational hotspot residue (R465, R479, R505 — WD40 substrate
                        recognition domain hotspots)
   Variant Allele Freq: 12% (tumor); 0% (normal) — confirmed somatic
   Coverage:            Tumor: 583x; Variant reads: 70
   Classification:      Variant of Uncertain Significance, likely loss-of-function (hotspot context)
   Functional Impact:   FBXW7 is the substrate-recognition component of an SCF-type ubiquitin E3 ligase complex
                        responsible for ubiquitin-mediated proteasomal degradation of key oncoproteins including
                        c-MYC, cyclin E, NOTCH1/2, c-JUN, and MCL-1. The p.R505C variant affects the WD40 beta-
                        propeller domain critical for substrate binding. The arginine residues at positions 465,
                        479, and 505 form a mutational hotspot cluster in HNSCC and other solid tumors; substitution
                        at these positions impairs substrate ubiquitination, resulting in accumulation of growth-
                        promoting oncoproteins. While p.R505C is less functionally characterized than p.R465C or
                        p.R479Q, the structural homology and oncoproteome stabilization context support likely
                        loss-of-function. FBXW7 mutations occur in approximately 5-10% of HNSCC.
   Clinical Relevance:  Not directly actionable with currently available targeted agents. Stabilization of MCL-1
                        downstream of FBXW7 loss may theoretically affect sensitivity to BCL-2/BCL-XL inhibitors;
                        this remains investigational.
   Associated Pathways: Ubiquitin-proteasome system, cell cycle regulation (cyclin E), NOTCH signaling, MYC stability

TP53:           No somatic mutation detected. This finding is EXPECTED and CONSISTENT with HPV-driven
                carcinogenesis: the HPV E6 oncoprotein mediates proteasomal degradation of wild-type p53
                protein, functionally inactivating the p53 pathway without requiring genomic mutation. The
                absence of TP53 mutation is a hallmark of HPV-positive HNSCC and distinguishes this tumor
                biologically from HPV-negative squamous cell carcinoma, in which TP53 mutation is present
                in >70% of cases.

CDKN2A/2B:     No homozygous deletion or point mutation detected. In HPV-positive HNSCC, the pRb pathway
                is functionally suppressed by the HPV E7 oncoprotein (which directly binds and inactivates
                pRb), obviating selective pressure for CDKN2A (p16) mutation or deletion. The strong
                nuclear/cytoplasmic p16 positivity on IHC (>70% of cells, block-positive pattern) reflects
                transcriptional upregulation of CDKN2A as a surrogate marker of active HPV E7 oncoprotein
                activity and is consistent with this molecular profile.

EGFR:          No amplification detected (copy number ratio 1.1). No activating point mutations in exons
                18-21 detected. EGFR amplification, when present in HNSCC, is more commonly associated with
                HPV-negative tumors. Absence of EGFR amplification is concordant with the HPV-positive
                molecular subtype.

FGFR1/2/3:     No FGFR1, FGFR2, or FGFR3 amplification detected. No FGFR fusion detected. No activating
                point mutations detected.

KRAS/NRAS/HRAS: No hotspot mutations detected in codons 12, 13, 61, or 146. RAS mutations are distinctly
                uncommon in HNSCC.

NOTCH1/2/3:    No pathogenic somatic alterations detected. NOTCH pathway alterations occur in a subset of
                HNSCC but were not identified in this specimen.

SOX2:          Focal gain at 3q26 locus detected (see copy number section); no point mutation.

PIK3R1:        No pathogenic mutation detected.

AKT1/2/3:      No activating mutations detected.

PTEN:          No deletion or inactivating mutation detected.

BRCA1/2:       No pathogenic somatic mutations detected. Germline BRCA analysis not performed on this
                report; if clinically indicated, reflex germline testing may be considered.

ATM:           No pathogenic somatic mutations detected.

NSD1:          No mutation detected. NSD1 mutations define a biologically distinct molecular subtype of
                HPV-negative HNSCC; its absence is concordant with HPV-positive status.

HRAS:          No mutation detected. HRAS mutations are more characteristic of oral cavity SCC.

This tumor demonstrates a molecular profile highly characteristic of HPV-positive oropharyngeal
squamous cell carcinoma. Key molecular features include:

(1) HPV-16 as the primary oncogenic driver, with confirmed chromosomal integration at 8q24, high
    viral load, and functional inactivation of the p53 and pRb tumor suppressor pathways via E6/E7
    oncoproteins (corroborated by the absence of TP53 mutation and absence of CDKN2A deletion,
    with strong p16 overexpression by IHC).

(2) PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway activation via PIK3CA p.E545K hotspot mutation (VAF 18%) occurring in
    the context of 3q amplification — a recurrent and co-occurring alteration in HPV-positive HNSCC
    that may be relevant if targeted therapy is considered in a recurrent/metastatic scenario.

(3) Immune pathway dysregulation via TRAF3 loss-of-function (p.R118*, VAF 22%), activating
    non-canonical NF-kappaB signaling, a pathway selectively enriched in HPV-positive HNSCC and
    potentially relevant to innate immune evasion and inflammatory tumor microenvironment remodeling.

(4) Chromatin remodeling pathway co-mutation involving both KMT2D (histone H3K4 methyltransferase,
    truncating, VAF 19%) and EP300 (histone acetyltransferase, missense, VAF 15%), reflecting a
    commonly disrupted epigenetic regulation axis in HNSCC.

(5) FBXW7 p.R505C (VAF 12%) — ubiquitin-mediated proteolysis pathway alteration with potential
    downstream effects on c-MYC, NOTCH, and cyclin E stability.

Overall genomic landscape: Low tumor mutational burden (4 mut/Mb), microsatellite stable, consistent
with a virally driven carcinogenesis mechanism. PD-L1 CPS of 18 may be relevant for immune checkpoint
inhibitor eligibility in the recurrent/metastatic setting. No high-confidence targetable alterations
beyond PIK3CA are identified; the PIK3CA p.E545K mutation warrants consideration for PI3K-directed
clinical trial enrollment if disease recurrence occurs.

DNA was extracted from FFPE tissue following macrodissection of tumor-enriched regions by a
board-certified pathologist. Hybrid capture-based targeted sequencing was performed using a
proprietary 500-gene panel encompassing all coding exons of cancer-relevant genes, select
intronic regions for fusion detection, and microsatellite repeat loci. Sequencing was performed
on an Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform. Bioinformatic analysis was performed using validated
pipelines for somatic SNV/indel calling (paired tumor-normal), CNV analysis (log2 ratio and
allelic imbalance), fusion detection, MSI quantification, and TMB calculation. Variants were
filtered against population databases (gnomAD, ExAC) and classified per AMP/ASCO/CAP guidelines
(2017, updated). All variants with VAF >5% and minimum 10 supporting reads were reviewed manually
by a molecular pathologist.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Okonkwo is a 52-year-old Nigerian-American woman, never-smoker, who presents for multidisciplinary evaluation and treatment planning of a newly diagnosed HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, T2N1M0, Stage I per AJCC 8th edition (HPV-positive oropharynx classification).

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo presented to her primary care physician approximately 11 weeks ago with a 6-week history of progressive left-sided throat discomfort and globus sensation. She described mild odynophagia and referred left-sided otalgia, which was particularly notable during swallowing. On that initial PCP visit, a firm, non-tender 2.5 cm lymph node was palpated in the left level II cervical chain. She was empirically treated with a 10-day course of amoxicillin-clavulanate for presumed pharyngitis. She returned approximately 5 weeks later with persistent symptoms and no reduction in the cervical lymphadenopathy, prompting referral to Otolaryngology.

Otolaryngology performed a thorough evaluation including flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy, which demonstrated a suspicious left base of tongue mass. A CT scan of the neck with contrast was obtained, showing a 2.2 cm enhancing mass at the left base of tongue with extension toward the left vallecula, and multiple left level II/IIA lymph nodes with the dominant node measuring 2.7 cm and demonstrating central necrosis — highly concerning for metastatic adenopathy. Directed biopsy of the left base of tongue mass was performed in clinic under local anesthesia. Pathology returned squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, with strong diffuse p16 positivity in a block-positive pattern (>70% of cells) and high-risk HPV RNA positivity by in situ hybridization. Keratinizing morphology was absent, consistent with HPV-driven pathogenesis.

For complete staging, the patient underwent a PET-CT scan, which confirmed an intensely FDG-avid left base of tongue primary (SUVmax 14.2) and a hypermetabolic left level IIA lymph node measuring 2.6 cm (SUVmax 11.8) with a central photopenic area consistent with necrosis. No contralateral cervical involvement, no retropharyngeal nodal disease, and no evidence of distant metastatic disease were identified. No synchronous primary was found. Fine needle aspiration of the left level IIA node under ultrasound guidance confirmed metastatic squamous cell carcinoma with p16-positive staining on cell block. Comprehensive next-generation sequencing (500-gene panel) was performed on the primary biopsy specimen and is notable for a PIK3CA p.E545K hotspot mutation (VAF 18%), TRAF3 p.R118* loss-of-function mutation (VAF 22%), EP300 p.R418C mutation (VAF 15%), FBXW7 p.R505C (VAF 12%), and KMT2D truncating mutation (VAF 19%). Tumor mutational burden is low at 4 mutations/Mb, microsatellite stable, PD-L1 CPS 18, and no TP53 mutation — the latter consistent with HPV-mediated functional p53 suppression via E6 protein. HPV integration was detected at chromosome 8q24. No actionable fusions or EGFR/FGFR amplifications identified.

Formal AJCC 8th edition (HPV-positive oropharynx) staging: T2N1M0, Stage I. The patient was referred to the multidisciplinary head and neck oncology program for definitive treatment planning and is presenting today for tumor board discussion and treatment recommendation.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Impression: Ms. Okonkwo is a 52-year-old never-smoking woman with newly diagnosed HPV-positive (p16+, HPV RNA ISH+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, AJCC 8th edition T2N1M0, Stage I (HPV-positive oropharynx classification), with a single ipsilateral necrotic level IIA metastatic lymph node. Her overall health is excellent (ECOG PS 0, normal organ function, BMI 24.1, no significant comorbidities). This is a locoregional disease with highly favorable HPV-positive biology.

Case was formally presented and discussed at today's Multidisciplinary Head and Neck Tumor Board. Attendees included Head and Neck Medical Oncology, Radiation Oncology, Head and Neck Surgery, Diagnostic Radiology, Pathology, Speech-Language Pathology, Nutrition/Dietetics, Dental Oncology, and Oncology Social Work.

1. TREATMENT RECOMMENDATION — DEFINITIVE CONCURRENT CHEMORADIATION:

After full multidisciplinary discussion, the consensus recommendation is definitive concurrent chemoradiation with intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) and cisplatin-based chemotherapy. This is the standard-of-care approach for this disease, supported by robust level 1 evidence from multiple phase III trials (including RTOG 0129, and the MACH-NC meta-analysis). The expected locoregional control and overall survival rates for HPV-positive stage I (T2N1) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma with concurrent chemoradiation are excellent, with 5-year disease-specific survival rates exceeding 85-90% in comparable cohorts.

Radiation: IMRT with simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique using volumetric modulated arc therapy (VMAT) with daily CBCT image guidance. The radiation oncology team has outlined a plan of 70 Gy to the high-risk PTV in 35 fractions, with elective nodal volumes at intermediate and low-risk doses. Detailed dosimetry and organ-at-risk constraints (parotid glands, spinal cord, brainstem, mandible, larynx) will be finalized at simulation.

Cisplatin: High-dose bolus cisplatin 100 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 22, and 43 of radiation (3 planned cycles), per RTOG/NRG standard. Pre-hydration with 3 L normal saline per cycle, with standard antiemetics (ondansetron, dexamethasone) and magnesium supplementation. Baseline renal function is adequate (creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, GFR >60 mL/min). Audiologic baseline is documented.

Rationale for standard-dose cisplatin (vs. weekly carboplatin or cetuximab): Given the patient's excellent performance status, normal renal function, baseline audiogram, and never-smoker status with HPV-positive disease, the established high-dose cisplatin regimen was favored for its superior locoregional control data. Substitution with cetuximab would be reserved for patients with significant cisplatin contraindications.

2. DE-ESCALATION CLINICAL TRIAL CONSIDERATION:

The tumor board discussed at length the patient's eligibility for available institutional and cooperative group de-escalation trials for HPV-positive low-risk oropharyngeal cancer (e.g., reduced-dose radiation or reduced-intensity chemotherapy). The favorable disease characteristics — HPV positivity, never-smoker, T2 primary, single ipsilateral node, complete response to be expected — make her a biologically appropriate candidate for de-escalation principles as explored in NRG-HN002, ORATOR2, and related trials. However, after careful review, the multidisciplinary team determined that the patient is ineligible for the current institutional de-escalation trial due to the presence of central nodal necrosis in the involved left level IIA lymph node, which is an explicit exclusion criterion on the open protocol. She will be treated with standard-of-care concurrent chemoradiation.

3. PEG TUBE AND NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT:

The speech-language pathology team presented the results of the baseline modified swallowing evaluation, which showed mild-to-moderate oropharyngeal dysphagia with penetration on thin liquids at baseline. Given the anticipated severity and duration of radiation-induced mucositis (70 Gy to the oropharynx with concurrent cisplatin), the nutrition team noted high risk for treatment-related weight loss and prolonged inability to maintain adequate oral intake. Prophylactic PEG tube placement was discussed in detail with the patient. The decision was made that the patient would undergo PEG tube placement prior to the initiation of chemoradiation, as a multidisciplinary decision, both for nutritional optimization during treatment and for safety given her pre-existing dysphagia. The patient was fully counseled on PEG tube care and enteral feeding protocols. Pre-treatment weight optimization via supplemental enteral nutrition will begin promptly.

4. DENTAL CLEARANCE AND RADIATION PLANNING:

Dental clearance has been completed with all necessary extractions and prophylaxis performed. Fluoride trays have been initiated. Radiation simulation CT will be scheduled imminently for treatment planning purposes.

5. SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT AND SUPPORTIVE CARE:

The patient will be closely monitored throughout treatment for mucositis (anticipated to be grade 2-3), radiation dermatitis, nausea/vomiting, nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, and fatigue. She will be seen weekly during radiation by both medical oncology and radiation oncology. A dedicated oncology dietitian will monitor weight and enteral feeding throughout. Oncology social work met with the patient today to establish support resources, including connecting her with the HPV-positive head and neck cancer support group and online patient resources.

6. MONITORING PARAMETERS:

- Weekly CBCs and basic metabolic panels during chemoradiation, with additional creatinine/electrolytes checked prior to each cisplatin dose
- Audiology re-check at cycle 3 (day 43)
- Serum magnesium monitoring given cisplatin-associated wasting
- Levothyroxine and thyroid function: TSH to be rechecked mid-treatment and post-treatment given risk of radiation-induced hypothyroidism
- Post-treatment response assessment PET-CT to be scheduled at 10-12 weeks post-completion of radiation to allow adequate resolution of treatment-related inflammatory changes

7. DISPOSITION:

The patient was seen today for her multidisciplinary consultation. She understands the diagnosis, staging, proposed treatment plan, anticipated toxicities, and rationale for standard-of-care versus de-escalation approaches. She expressed clear understanding and wishes to proceed with the recommended treatment. Informed consent for concurrent chemoradiation will be obtained separately by radiation oncology and medical oncology. PEG tube placement will be coordinated with interventional gastroenterology this week. Radiation simulation will be scheduled as soon as PEG placement is completed and the patient has healed.

Patient voiced appreciation for the thorough multidisciplinary process and expressed particular concern about maintaining her ability to speak and teach post-treatment. She was reassured that HPV-positive oropharyngeal carcinoma treated with this regimen carries an excellent prognosis and that the speech-language pathology team will work with her throughout and after treatment to optimize functional recovery.

Follow-up: Treatment to begin within approximately 2 weeks. Patient will be seen by radiation oncology for simulation and by medical oncology for pre-chemoradiation counseling and cycle 1 planning visit before first treatment day.
